ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><pBdr><bottom val="single" sz="4" color="1F3864" /></pBdr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="1F3864" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="4" /><szCs val="4" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="4" /><szCs val="4" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve" /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="36F6C5D2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFFFFF" /><spacing before="60" after="60" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve" /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="EAE7C582" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFFFFF" /><spacing before="60" after="60" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve" /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0AE7642C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="000B00BD" rsidRDefault="000B00BD"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFFFFF" /><spacing before="60" after="60" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><color val="000000" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /></rPr></pPr></p><p ns2:paraId="69FA9664" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00CE6E60" rsidP="00CE6E60"><pPr><pStyle val="Style1" /></pPr><r><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>03. ACUTE CORONARY SYNDROME &amp; HYPERLIPIDEMIA</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4D687330" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>A. Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3B17D1C1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Pathophysiology &amp; Types</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1BD6A05F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Umbrella term for Unstable Angina (UA), Non-ST-Elevation MI (NSTEMI), and ST-Elevation MI (STEMI).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="13D32C63" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Typically caused by reduced O2 supply (plaque rupture). </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Nearly 50% of occlusive thrombi develop at sites with &lt; 50% luminal narrowing</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> (these young, inflammatory plaques have thin fibrous caps and are vulnerable).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="275A9D92" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Other causes: Normal coronary arteries with spasm (Prinzmetal’s angina, transient ST elevation), Kawasaki disease, or Cocaine-induced. </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Cocaine Treatment Pearl: Treat with NTG and CCB; strictly avoid Beta-Blockers</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5A5BA814" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Diagnostic Evaluation</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="094BF065" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">UA and NSTEMI present identically (both can show ST depression or T-wave inversion). They are differentiated </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>only</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> by biomarkers (Troponin release in NSTEMI).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="41520019" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>ST Segment Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> ST depression in inferior/lateral leads indicates ischemia but does </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> reliably correlate to the specific anatomic vessel involved.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="01DA9BC6" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Biomarkers: Troponin I and T are equivalent. Detected 3-6 hours after onset; remains elevated for 7-14 days. Normal serial troponins exclude MI, but do </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> exclude Unstable Angina. CK-MB and myoglobin are generally no longer used, except CK-MB can be helpful to diagnose re-infarction a week later.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3A96A27E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>NSTEMI / UA Management (2014 AHA/ACC Guidelines)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3FE49205" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Ischemia-Guided vs. Early Invasive Strategy. BOTH get:</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3EC296EB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Aspirin (non-enteric coated, chewable 162-325 mg for rapid absorption).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4A248BB0" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>P2Y12 inhibitor (Clopidogrel, Ticagrelor).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="770FC25D" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Anticoagulation (Heparin).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="32A06B6A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Early Invasive Strategy adds a Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptor blocker (Tirofiban, Eptifibatide, Abciximab) before catheterization.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4F457E46" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Initial invasive strategy is recommended for: recurrent symptoms despite meds, previous PCI/CABG, heart failure, serious arrhythmias, LV EF &lt; 0.40, large perfusion defects, high-risk TIMI/GRACE score, or markedly elevated troponin.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="380A292F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>STEMI Diagnosis &amp; Management</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="78DABD2C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>EKG changes: ST elevation occurs immediately. Resolution suggests reperfusion. Persistent elevation suggests ventricular aneurysm. Q waves develop at ~12 hours and indicate permanently dead myocardium; you cannot date an MI solely by Q waves without prior EKGs.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7A983DA9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>EKG Territories: Anterior/LAD (V1-V4), Lateral/Circumflex (V5-V6), Inferior/RCA (II, III, aVF).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3D828630" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>LBBB Nuance:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> LBBB masks EKG changes. Sgarbossa criteria are used to determine need for reperfusion, but routine prophylactic cath for a new LBBB is no longer automatic standard of care.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5362BAC6" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t xml:space="preserve">Treatment: Reperfusion ideally within 12 hours. PCI is preferred. Fibrinolytics are indicated </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>only</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> if time to transport to PCI-capable hospital is &gt;12 hours, or if time from first medical contact at a non-PCI hospital to device time is &gt;2 hours.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0DA427E2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Post-Intervention Pharmacology</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6D9AB66E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Bare Metal (BMS) vs. Drug-Eluting Stents (DES): BMS acts as a foreign body causing in-stent thrombosis, leading to rapid endothelialization causing in-stent stenosis. DES delays endothelialization, keeping the metal exposed longer.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6355960A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>DAPT Rules: Aspirin 81 mg + P2Y12 Inhibitor.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6569F60A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>ACS with BMS or DES: DAPT for at least 12 months.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2D10D9DE" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Stable CAD (scheduled cath) with BMS: DAPT for at least 1 month.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7A82D344" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Stable CAD (scheduled cath) with DES: DAPT for at least 6 months.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="07EFCBB2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>P2Y12 Inhibitor Nuances:</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="15662609" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Clopidogrel (Plavix): Prodrug. Metabolized by CYP2C19. Efficacy reduced by concomitant 2C19 inhibitors (fluconazole, fluoxetine, omeprazole).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0ACDEDDB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Prasugrel (Effient): Prodrug. More efficient conversion without CYP genetic variations.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="192AA215" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Ticagrelor (Brilinta): Not a prodrug. Provides the most consistently high platelet inhibition. (Effectiveness roughly maps alphabetically: C -&gt; P -&gt; T).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1398C9F6" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">CABG Indications: Left main stenosis, 3-vessel disease with EF &lt;50%, or 2/3-vessel disease with &gt;75% proximal LAD stenosis. </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Board Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Diabetic patients have better 8-year survival with CABG than PCI. Require DAPT for &gt;1 year post-CABG to prevent graft occlusion.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="67889D09" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="156" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Post-MI Survival Drugs: Only four drug classes improve survival post-MI: ACEi/ARB, Beta-Blockers, Statins, and Aspirin. Nitrates, CCBs, and Digoxin strictly improve symptoms but do </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> improve mortality.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7EFD44B9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CE6E60" rsidRDefault="00CE6E60" rsidP="00CE6E60"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr></p><p ns2:paraId="554260F8" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CE6E60" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00CE6E60" rsidP="00CE6E60"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr></p><p ns2:paraId="6E8EFC5B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>B. Pericarditis</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7E742F55" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="83" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Common in young adults (viral/idiopathic). Sharp chest pain relieved by leaning forward. Pericardial friction rub present. EKG shows diffuse ST elevation.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4A48CEE2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="83" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Treatment is strictly inflammatory: NSAIDs. Indomethacin is the gold standard but carries high GI upset risk. High-dose aspirin (2-4 g/day) is an alternative.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="66358971" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>C. Hyperlipidemia Management (ACC/AHA)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2EE6AFFC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>The Four Statin Benefit Groups &amp; Intensity</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="62FA1AA8" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Instead of treating to an absolute LDL number, treat based on risk category.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2FDD7D24" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Group 1: Individuals with clinical ASCVD.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5F16AD4F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Age &lt; 75 = High-intensity statin. Age &gt; 75 = Moderate-intensity statin.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0403AABC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>Group 2: Primary elevation of LDL &gt; 190 mg/dL (suggests familial hyperlipidemia).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1BB9AB84" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Treat with High-intensity statin regardless of age.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3CF5A9CA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Group 3: Diabetics aged 40-75 with LDL 70-189.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="377F851F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>10-year risk &gt; 7.5% = High-intensity. Risk &lt; 7.5% = Moderate-intensity.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3222CED4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Group 4: No ASCVD or DM, age 40-75, LDL 70-189.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7C656950" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Estimated 10-year risk &gt; 7.5% = Moderate-to-High intensity statin. (Note: USPSTF uses a 10% threshold).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="59861B25" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Statin Pharmacologic Dosing</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="08CC48AA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>High-Intensity (&gt;50% LDL reduction): Atorvastatin 40-80 mg, Rosuvastatin 20 mg.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="200C5CA7" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Moderate-Intensity (30 to &lt;50% LDL reduction): Atorvastatin 10-20 mg, Rosuvastatin 5-10 mg, Simvastatin 20-40 mg, Pravastatin 40-80 mg, Lovastatin 40 mg, Fluvastatin 40 mg BID.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="154B43AB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00B71DEE" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Statins provide an approximate 30% relative risk reduction in CV events regardless of starting risk.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="036FB82A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CE6E60" rsidRPr="00CE6E60" rsidRDefault="00CE6E60" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr></p><p ns2:paraId="6FF8C9D8" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00B71DEE" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00B71DEE" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr></p><p ns2:paraId="36645237" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Risk Stratification Tools &amp; Labs</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="41B2CD70" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>ASCVD Calculator:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Uses Sys BP, BP meds, smoking, Total Chol, HDL, DM, Age, Gender, Race. Tends to have very good discrimination but fair calibration (overestimates numerical risk).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="03802A24" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Risk Enhancing Factors:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Family history premature ASCVD (M&lt;55, F&lt;65), sustained LDL &gt;160, Metabolic syndrome, CKD, preeclampsia/early menopause (&lt;40), Triglycerides &gt;175, hs-CRP &gt;2.0, ABI &lt;0.9, Lipoprotein(a) &gt;50, apoB &gt;130.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="66DD627F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Coronary Artery Calcium (CAC):</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Useful for intermediate-risk (7.5-19.9%). CAC=0 (favor withholding statin unless DM/Smoker/Fam Hx), CAC 1-99 (favors statin if &gt;55), CAC &gt;100 (initiate statin). </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Statins accelerate plaque calcification while stabilizing the cap; CAC scoring is invalid/useless in patients already taking a statin.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="56187D8C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Lab Nuance:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Total Cholesterol and HDL are fully accurate in a non-fasting state. Friedewald calculated LDL is inaccurate if Triglycerides are &gt;400 or LDL &lt;70.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="049554C8" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Step-Up Non-Statin Therapies</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6FE225DD" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>If a patient is at very-high risk and on maximally tolerated statin with LDL persistently &gt; 70 mg/dL:</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="51D38BBE" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Step 1: Add Ezetimibe (10 mg daily). Ezetimibe drops LDL by an additional 25-30%. Adding Ezetimibe to a moderate statin approximates high-intensity monotherapy outcomes.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3BA4C6A0" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Step 2: If LDL &gt;70 or non-HDL &gt;100 on Statin + Ezetimibe, add a PCSK9 inhibitor (Evolocumab 140 mg SQ q2w, Alirocumab) or Inclisiran (siRNA).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3B64B05B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Omega-3s &amp; Statin Myopathy</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="20CE70CC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Omega-3s generally show no CVD event reduction, </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>except</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Icosapent Ethyl (Vascepa) 2g BID (REDUCE-IT Trial), which reduced CV death by 0.9% but increased Atrial Fibrillation/Flutter risk by 1%.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2AC9FE81" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="160" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>Statin Myopathy classifications: Myalgia (normal CK), Myositis (elevated CK), Rhabdomyolysis (elevated CK + elevated Creatinine). Discontinue statin strictly if CK &gt; 10x upper limit of normal (ULN).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="22355DC7" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><pict ns2:anchorId="136388D3"><ns3:rect /><color val="FFFFFF" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  AAFP Poll Questions — Session 04: Hypertension</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="0" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="7F4F24" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  4 practice questions from the AAFP Board Prep presentation. Work each question before checking the answer key.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  1.  A 48-year-old female presents to clinic as a new patient. BP 172/110 in both arms; BMI 24.4; CV exam unremarkable. Medications: none, including no OTC supplements BMP: Na 144 mEq/L, K 3.3 mEq/L, Cr 0.68 mg/dL. If the patient’s hypertension should prove refractory to treatment, which one of the following tests is most likely to reveal the cause of her elevated blood pressure?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  24-hour urine catecholamine level</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Plasma aldosterone/renin ratio</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  MRA of the renal arteries</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Sleep study (polysomnography)</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 04</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  2.  A 58-year-old woman has home BP of 155/95 mm Hg, confirmed by multiple similar readings and office BP of 154/94. She exercises, follows a low-salt diet, and rarely drinks alcohol. Which one of the following medications would be most appropriate as initial therapy for this patient?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Chlorthalidone</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Clonidine (Catapres)</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Doxazosin (Cardura)</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Metoprolol succinate (Toprol-XL)</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 04</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  3.  A 62-year-old man presents for follow-up of HTN. Despite adherence to his daily regimen of chlorthalidone, carvedilol, amlodipine, and lisinopril, his BP averages 152/92 mm Hg. Recent labs are normal, including CBC, BMP, UA. Which one of the following medication adjustments would be most appropriate to bring his blood pressure to goal?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Change chlorthalidone to furosemide</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Change lisinopril to losartan</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Add isosorbide mononitrate</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Add spironolactone</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 04</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  4.  A 57-year-old male with type 2 diabetes has a blood pressure of 148/94 and creatinine of 1.25 mg/dL. One month after starting lisinopril 20 mg/d, his blood pressure is 128/80 and creatinine has increased to 1.6 mg/dL. A repeat creatinine 1 week later is unchanged. What is the best approach in this situation?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Continue lisinopril at the same dosage</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Reduce the lisinopril dosage to 10 mg</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Change lisinopril to chlorthalidone</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Change lisinopril to losartan</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 04</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="40" after="120" /><shd val="clear" fill="7F4F24" color="auto" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4626FE4B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFF2CC" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  [★ MUST-READ]  Smith DK, Lennon RP, Carlsgaard PB: Managing hypertension using combination therapy. Am Fam Physician 2020;101(6):341-349.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="F45BC650" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFF2CC" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Cited by: Q2025-187, Q2022-437, Q2020-071</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="02000F68" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="EBF3FB" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  [CORE]  Charles L, Triscott J, Dobbs B. Secondary hypertension: discovering the underlying cause. Am Fam Physician. 2017;96(7):453-461.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="62CA52D6" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="EBF3FB" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Cited by: Q2023-699, Q2022-401</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="FE6D8997" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="EBF3FB" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  [CORE]  Funder JW, Carey RM, Mantero F, et al: The management of primary aldosteronism: Case detection, diagnosis, and treatment: An Endocrine Society clinical practice guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2016;101(5):1889-1916.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7CCCA20A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="EBF3FB" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Cited by: Q2022-401</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="03D37DBD" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><pBdr><bottom val="single" sz="4" color="1F3864" /></pBdr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="1F3864" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="4" /><szCs val="4" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="4" /><szCs val="4" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve" /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="B82091CC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFFFFF" /><spacing before="60" after="60" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve" /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="8865C2C2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFFFFF" /><spacing before="60" after="60" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve" /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7999DF78" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="000B00BD" rsidRDefault="000B00BD"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFFFFF" /><spacing before="60" after="60" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><color val="000000" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /></rPr></pPr></p><p ns2:paraId="1F1EF61B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00CE6E60" rsidP="00CE6E60"><pPr><pStyle val="Style1" /></pPr><r><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>04. HYPERTENSION</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="01D438DD" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>A. Pathophysiology &amp; Diagnosis</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="45961B99" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="211" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Cardiovascular Risk</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2AADA7F5" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="211" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Log-linear relationship: From a baseline "normal" of 115/75 mmHg, every 20 mmHg systolic or 10 mmHg diastolic increase doubles the risk of major CV events and stroke.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="631137A5" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="211" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Diagnostic Evaluation</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2690A662" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="211" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Ambulatory BP Monitoring (ABPM) is recommended to confirm a diagnosis and has a stronger association with CVD outcomes than clinic measurements.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="164BD798" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="211" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Out-of-office screening differentiates:</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="769A9B76" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="211" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>White coat hypertension:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Elevated in clinic, normal at home (minimal CVD risk increase).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="244AF561" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="211" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Masked hypertension:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Normal in clinic, elevated at home (up to 35% prevalence, carries CVD risk similar to sustained HTN). Often caused by short-acting antihypertensives (e.g., atenolol) wearing off overnight.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6AB45949" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="211" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Pediatric Hypertension</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="23D23790" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="211" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Defined as  &gt; 95th percentile for age, gender, and height based on AAP tables. Routine annual BP checks start at age 3.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="52AA5452" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="211" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Primary HTN due to obesity is the #1 cause in the US. Do </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> run extensive secondary workups (renal imaging, catechols) if the child is obese or has a strong family history.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6B71B2CA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="211" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Secondary Hypertension Causes</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="03E4E9E5" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="211" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Sleep Apnea: Now the leading secondary cause of HTN. Present in 30-40% of HTN patients and 60-70% of those with resistant HTN.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7FEDA910" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="211" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Primary Hyperaldosteronism: Present in 5-10% of general HTN, 7-20% of resistant HTN. Unprovoked hypokalemia supports diagnosis but is not strictly required. Diagnosed via early morning Aldosterone:Renin Ratio (patient must be off RAAS meds for 2 weeks).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5B7BD7D4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="211" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Renal Artery Stenosis: Age &lt; 30 = fibromuscular dysplasia; Age &gt; 30 = atherosclerotic. Clues include flash pulmonary edema or significant Creatinine bump on ACEi/ARB. Diagnosed by MRA (gold standard) or renal Doppler. </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Treatment Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Revascularization does not improve outcomes in the general population.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3913B429" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="211" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Other: Exogenous substances (NSAIDs, OCPs, Caffeine, Sympathomimetics).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="43F3620F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>B. Treatment Guidelines &amp; Pharmacology</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6B05B921" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Guideline Disputes (JNC 8 vs ACC/AHA 2017)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="559C2577" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>JNC 8 (2014): Goal is &lt;140/90 for general pop &lt;60 years, CKD, and Diabetics. Goal is relaxed to &lt;150/90 for general pop &gt;60 years.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="62563772" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>ACC/AHA (2017): Driven heavily by the SPRINT trial. Lowers threshold to 130/80 for anyone with clinical CVD, 10-year ASCVD &gt;10%, DM, CKD, or &gt;65 years old.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2099FBDD" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>Administrative Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> The AAFP specifically decided </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> to endorse the 2017 ACC/AHA guidelines, citing lack of systematic review, over-reliance on the SPRINT trial, conflicts of interest by the panel chair, and the fact it would diagnose 46% of the US with HTN overnight.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4E6012E5" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Initial Drug Selection</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="56C1AB37" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>General Non-Black (including DM):</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Thiazide, CCB, ACEi, or ARB.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5ED23685" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>General Black (including DM):</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Thiazide or CCB. Monotherapy with ACEi/ARB has reduced BP response and a 2-4x higher risk of angioedema. </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>ALLHAT Trial:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Thiazides better than ACEi for HF/CV outcomes in Black populations; CCBs better for stroke reduction.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4182687A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>CKD Population (&gt;18 yo):</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Initial or add-on treatment </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>must</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> include an ACEi or ARB to improve kidney outcomes regardless of race or diabetes status. </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Rule: Never prescribe an ACEi and ARB together.</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="42EEFA32" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Thiazide &amp; Thiazide-Like Diuretics</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="65011CE4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Metabolic Effects: Reduce excretion of Calcium (slows bone demineralization) and Uric acid (exacerbates gout). Increases excretion of Potassium (advise dietary salt restriction to minimize) and Magnesium (complicates hypo-K correction).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="76BE30AA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Glucose/Lipids: Average glucose increase is 3-5 mg/dL; DM is </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> a contraindication. Mild TG increase.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="795DE1CA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Efficacy Threshold: Ineffective when GFR &lt; 30-40 mL/min. Exception: Metolazone, which is used strictly in conjunction with loop diuretics.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="21AC024A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Dosing limits:</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5EE086C1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ): Max 25 mg daily. 50 mg is more effective but increases risk of sudden death.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="669E8AF4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Chlorthalidone: 12.5–25 mg initial; Max 50 mg. The preferred agent due to ALLHAT/SPRINT outcomes data.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6B45A19B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Indapamide: 1.25–2.5 mg initial; Max 5 mg. Good for "milligram-phobic" patients.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6955B7E2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="007D753C" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="007D753C" rsidP="007D753C"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><ind left="2160" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr></p><p ns2:paraId="187C4D9E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>ACEi / ARBs</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="73BA6B2E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>ACEi cough (10-20%) is due to bradykinin accumulation.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="51AEBA2C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Angioedema is not an allergic reaction (steroids do not help). If an ACEi causes angioedema requiring ICU/intubation, do not trial an ARB. Otherwise, ARB risk is tiny.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="37290A91" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Increases Lithium levels.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3C873BAF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Maximum Doses: Fosinopril 80mg, Lisinopril 80mg, Quinapril 80mg, Ramipril 20mg. Candesartan 32mg, Irbesartan 300mg, Losartan 100mg, Olmesartan 40mg, Valsartan 320mg.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2B098EB4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Calcium Channel Blockers &amp; Beta Blockers</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="28B458F9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>CCB Dihydropyridines (-dipine): Vasodilators with no negative AV node effects.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="29C60A2C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>CCB Nondihydropyridines (Verapamil &gt; Diltiazem): Slow AV conduction and have negative inotropic effects. Avoid in heart failure. Verapamil is highly constipating.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2D80F8ED" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>Rule:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Avoid short-acting CCBs (immediate nifedipine) as they increase mortality.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="47DA65C1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="1" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Beta-Blockers: Only three are approved for Heart Failure: Metoprolol succinate (max 400 mg/d), Carvedilol (max 50 mg/d), Bisoprolol (max 10 mg/d). BBs are no longer first-line for primary HTN due to inferior stroke reduction, but are used specifically in stable ischemic heart disease.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="207E65CC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>C. Refractory States &amp; Complications</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2C13FDEE" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="131" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Resistant Hypertension Step-Up</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="16015D7E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="131" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Defined as persistent HTN despite  &gt; 3 drugs in adequate doses, where </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>one drug must be a diuretic</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>. (Note: 12.5 mg HCTZ is inadequate; requires 25 mg Chlorthalidone).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4D6C6FEF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="131" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Most common cause is poor adherence or exogenous drugs.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="540EF066" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="131" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Drug #4 / Step-Up: The next add-on </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>must</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> be an Aldosterone Antagonist (Spironolactone 12.5-50 mg or Eplerenone 25-50 mg), even if hyperaldosteronism is not present. Excellent for OSA patients. Check GFR (&gt;30) and K (&lt;4.5) before initiation.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5C296B52" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="131" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Further add-ons: Vasodilating beta-blocker (carvedilol/labetalol block both alpha/beta; nebivolol increases nitric oxide), clonidine, or an alpha-blocker.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3C0702B4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="131" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Treatment-Induced Renal Decline</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="73FF8C07" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="131" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">When initiating an ACEi/ARB, efferent arteriole dilation reduces intraglomerular (IG) pressure. A resultant 20-30% rise in Creatinine that subsequently stabilizes is a </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>hemodynamic</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> response, not structural damage. This lowered IG pressure protects against long-term glomerulosclerosis.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2DABE42D" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="131" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Board Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Continue the ACEi/ARB at the same dosage unless the Creatinine increases by &gt;30%. Only if the spike is &gt;30% should you discontinue the agent and evaluate for bilateral renal artery stenosis.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="365D6119" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="131" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Hypertensive Emergencies vs. Severe Asymptomatic HTN</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3762D240" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="131" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Hypertensive Emergency: BP elevation </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>with</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> end-organ damage (aortic dissection, acute pulm edema, ACS, AKI, encephalopathy, eclampsia). Requires hospitalization.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6528B6BE" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="131" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Asymptomatic Severe HTN (formerly "urgency"): Managed outpatient without precipitous drops.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="65F77A87" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="131" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Treatment goals in Emergency: Avoid precipitous drops (can cause "steal" syndrome and infarction) using rapid-onset/short-duration titratable agents.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="62246F46" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="131" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Exceptions:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> "Permissive hypertension" in acute stroke (target SBP &lt;180). In aortic dissection, rapidly drop SBP to 100-110 mmHg.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2ACDAF4A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>D. Non-Pharmacologic Therapy &amp; Murmur Review</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2FCA8213" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="77" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Lifestyle</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="06ECE4BA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="77" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t xml:space="preserve">Weight loss, DASH diet, sodium reduction. Max 2 standard drinks/day for men; 1/day for women. </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>No evidence</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> for Ca/Mg supplements, relaxation therapy, yoga, or acupuncture.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="490F7488" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="77" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Systolic vs Diastolic Murmurs</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="70F671C0" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="77" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Systolic (Valves open in systole are stenotic; closed in systole are regurgitant): Aortic Stenosis (Right 2nd ICS, crescendo-decrescendo radiating to neck. Valve velocity, not area, drives replacement (TAVR). Statins do </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> slow progression). Pulmonic Stenosis (Left 2nd ICS). Mitral Regurg (Apex). Tricuspid Regurg (Left lower sternal border).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3976C59E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="77" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Bicuspid Aortic Valve: Autosomal dominant (1-2% prevalence). Must screen 1st degree relatives with echo.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1C880369" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="77" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Diastolic: Aortic Regurg (Left sternal border decrescendo; intensity increases by sitting up, leaning forward, holding breath after expiration). Mitral Stenosis (Apex, left lateral decubitus).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="28F791C9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00B71DEE" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> </t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3CA0A02A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00586D48" rsidRDefault="00586D48" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos"
