Despite treatment with a topical agent, they are essentially unchanged. These areas should now be treated with which of the following?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Benzathine penicillin G</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Pimecrolimus (Elidel)</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Oral fluconazole (Diflucan)</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Prednisone</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 09</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  7.  The most likely cause of his lesions is: Molluscum Contagiosum Courtesy of G Levine, MD Genital Warts Courtesy of the CDC Warts • Due to infection with HPV (Human Papillomavirus) • 150 types • HPV type 1 – plantar warts • HPV types 6 &amp; 11 – genital warts • Infection with HPV occurs by skin-to-skin contact Genital Wart—Treatment • Patient administered • Podofilox – 0.5% • BID x 3 days, off 4 days, repeat cycle x 4 • Imiquimod – 3.75% or 5% cream (Aldara) • HS, 3x/week, x 16 wks • Sinecatechins 15% ointment (Veregen) • TID for maximum of 16 wks Genital Wart—Treatment • Provider-administered • Cryotherapy • BCA/TCA • Surgical or laser removal Common Warts © Dr. Richard Usatine Plantar Wart Jefferson Clinical Images Database. http://jeffline.jefferson.edu/JCI/. Callus Jefferson Clinical Images Database. http://jeffline.jefferson.edu/JCI/. Duct Tape on Wart Source: Joel Franusic/Flickr Genital Herpes Courtesy of the CDC Genital Herpes—Treatment • Acyclovir (Zovirax) • Primary = 400 mg TID x 7-10 days • Recurrent = 800 mg BID x 5 days, 800 mg TID x 2 days • Suppression = 400 mg BID • Famciclovir • Primary = 250 mg TID x 7-10 days • Recurrent=125 mg BID x 5 days,1 g BID x 1 day, 500 mg then 250 mg BID x 2 days • Suppression = 250 mg BID Genital Herpes—Treatment • Valacyclovir (Valtrex) •Primary = 1 g BID x 7-10 days •Recurrent = 1 g daily x 5 days, 500 mg BID x 3 days •Suppression = 500 mg or 1 g daily Molluscum Contagiosum © Dr. Richard Usatine Molluscum Contagiosum • Caused by double-stranded DNA poxvirus • Spread by skin-to-skin contact and autoinoculation • Umbilicated, firm, flesh-colored, dome-shaped papules • Children • Lesions anywhere except palms &amp; soles • Treatment is optional • Adults • Lesions mostly in genital area • Should be treated to avoid sexual transmission Molluscum Contagiosum—Treatment • Benign Neglect - may spontaneously resolve in 6 months - 4 yrs • Cryosurgery • Curettage • May cause scarring • Cantharidin (non genital) • Imiquimod 5% cream (Aldara) • TID x 5 days/wkx 1 month • Podofilox (adults) • Cimetidine (children) • KOH 5% • Topical retinoids • Salicylic acid • Laser Case # 8 © Dr. Richard Usatine Case # 8 •A 68 yo male was sent in by his daughter who was concerned about a small growth on her father’s scalp. © Dr. Richard Usatine</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Treponema pallidum</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Herpes simplex virus</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Human papilloma virus</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Molluscum contagiosum virus</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 09</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  8.  Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Keratoacanthoma</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Dermatofibroma</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Sebaceous hyperplasia</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Basal cell carcinoma</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 09</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  9.  Which of the following would be the most reasonable course of action?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Treatment with topical 5-fluorouracil</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Observation of lesions</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Excisional biopsy with wide 4 cm margins</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Referral for Mohs micrographic surgery</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 09</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  10. /></pPr><r><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>09. DERMATOLOGY &amp; CUTANEOUS PROBLEMS</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5219A8C7" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>A. Plaques and Scales</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3D6CB388" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="257" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Psoriasis</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0E304F10" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="257" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Presentation: Oval, erythematous, plaque-like lesions typically on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees) and scalp. Associated with pitting fingernails and asymmetric polyarthritis.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6E55C671" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="257" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Topical Treatment (&lt; 5% body involvement): Corticosteroids, Calcipotriene (Dovonex - Vitamin D3 analog), Calcitriol, Calcineurin inhibitors (Tacrolimus/Pimecrolimus), Tazarotene, Coal tar, or Salicylic acid.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6BAACEA4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="257" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Systemic Treatment (&gt; 5% involvement / severe): Narrow-band UVB, PUVA, Methotrexate, Acitretin, Cyclosporine, Apremilast (Otezla), or Anti-TNF biologic agents (Infliximab, Etanercept, Adalimumab).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="12C65182" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="257" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Pityriasis Rosea: Characterized by an initial "Herald Patch" followed by scaly lesions in a Christmas-tree distribution.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5D31994E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>B. Bullous Lesions</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="52D31E33" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="232" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Herpes Zoster (Shingles)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="083A74B4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="232" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Presentation: Vesicles of varying sizes on an erythematous base in a single dermatome. Often preceded by localized burning, fever, and itching before the rash erupts.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="459BDFA1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="232" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Acute Treatment: Valacyclovir, Acyclovir, or Famciclovir.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="07DEECD9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="232" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Post-Herpetic Neuralgia (PHN) Treatment: Pregabalin, Gabapentin, Tricyclic antidepressants, or Sympathetic nerve blocks (Bupivacaine—must be administered within 2 months of onset to be effective). </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Board Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Oral corticosteroids (Prednisone) do </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> prevent or effectively treat PHN.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0E30EDA4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="232" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Bullous Pemphigoid vs. Pemphigus Vulgaris</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="061977E3" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="232" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Bullous Pemphigoid: Tense, non-painful bullae typically seen in the elderly. Pushing on the blister does </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> cause the tissue to slough off (Negative Nikolsky sign).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1A18E1A3" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="232" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Pemphigus Vulgaris: Pushing on the blister causes it to spread and the epidermis to shear off (Positive Nikolsky sign). Requires aggressive intervention.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4794D719" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>C. Pruritic Papules &amp; Infestations</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="340CAC5B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="3" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Scabies (Sarcoptes scabiei)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="653CE377" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="3" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Presentation: Intense nocturnal pruritus. Curved or linear burrows in finger webs, wrists, elbows, axilla, and genitals.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6BEF4636" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="3" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Diagnosis: Locate burrow with felt-tip pen ink, scrape with a #15 curved scalpel blade, view under mineral oil or KOH. Look for mites, eggs, and scybala (fecal pellets).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="55657ABB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="3" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>First-Line Treatment: 5% Permethrin cream.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="31222E2D" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="3" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>Severe/Norwegian (Crusted) Scabies: Overwhelming infestation often seen in immunocompromised patients; not particularly pruritic. Treat with Oral Ivermectin (200 mcg/kg).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="075250D0" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="3" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Environmental Rules: Launder all bedding/clothes worn within 72 hours in hot water, dry clean, or place in a sealed plastic bag for 3 days. Must treat patient, intimate contacts, and all household members simultaneously.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7BF2F494" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="3" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Pediculosis Capitis (Head Lice)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="329DDABC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="3" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Treatment Options: Pyrethroids (1st line: Permethrin 1% or Pyrethrins), Malathion 0.5% lotion, Benzyl alcohol 5%, Spinosad 0.9% topical, or Topical Ivermectin 0.5%. Use Oral Ivermectin for resistant cases.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6CDD39A2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="3" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Administrative Pearl: According to the CDC, children with pediculosis capitis do </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> need to be excluded from school once treatment has started. Prophylactically treat individuals who share bedding.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7DF8E5CC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="3" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Hot Tub Folliculitis</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5094BD51" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="3" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Caused by </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Pseudomonas aeruginosa</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>. Presents as a papular/pustular rash after hot tub exposure.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="752FB027" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="3" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Usually self-limited requiring no pharmacologic therapy. If severe, treat with an oral fluoroquinolone (Ciprofloxacin).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4185BF4E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>D. Acne Vulgaris &amp; Rosacea</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4308E5FB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="255" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Acne Vulgaris</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="476BB56F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="255" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Pathophysiology: Androgen-mediated stimulation of sebum and keratinocytes causes a plug, allowing </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Cutibacterium acnes</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> to proliferate and trigger inflammation. Lesions range from comedones (open=blackheads, closed=whiteheads) to pustules and cysts.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0A1C48B5" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="255" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Treatment Algorithm: Topical retinoids -&gt; add Topical Benzoyl Peroxide -&gt; add Topical Clindamycin -&gt; add Oral Antibiotics (Doxycycline) -&gt; Oral Isotretinoin (for severe nodulocystic disease).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7A1DF203" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="255" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Rosacea</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="69DA4803" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="255" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Primary Features: Erythema (transient flushing), Papules/Pustules, and Telangiectasia. </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Complication:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Rhinophyma (bulbous hypertrophy of the nose).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="76097CB8" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="255" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Triggers: Extremes of temperature, sunlight, spicy foods, alcohol, exercise, menopausal hot flashes, and vasodilating medications (e.g., Niacin).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="650292AE" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>E. Hypopigmented Lesions &amp; Fungal Infections</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2EC21685" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="98" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Tinea Versicolor</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="66E6C913" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Malassezia</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> species.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3EBCD929" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="98" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Treatment: Topical selenium sulfide, Ketoconazole 2% shampoo, or Oral Fluconazole (Diflucan).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6DE3CC03" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="98" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Board Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Strictly avoid oral Ketoconazole due to hepatotoxicity risks.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5716924A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="98" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>Prophylaxis: Ketoconazole 2% shampoo weekly, or Itraconazole 200 mg PO BID once a month for 6 months.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="17F6FCD3" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="98" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Erythrasma</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="794DD941" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="98" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Bacterial infection (</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Corynebacterium minutissimum</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>) occurring in intertriginous areas (groin, axilla, inframammary).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3A949211" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="98" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Diagnosis: Wood's lamp examination produces a pathognomonic coral pink/red fluorescence.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="70B46EF7" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="98" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Treatment: Topical or oral macrolides.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5511DCEF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="98" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Tinea Capitis</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2955A98C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="98" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Board Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Cannot be treated topically. Must be treated with systemic oral antifungals (Griseofulvin or Terbinafine). Severe inflammatory presentations are termed a "Kerion".</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="565628D7" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>F. Pigmented &amp; Nodular Lesions (Dermato-oncology)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="426996CC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Malignant Melanoma</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="67CAF004" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Risk Factors: Non-Hispanic Caucasians in their 50s-60s, Men &gt; Women. Watch for the "Ugly Duckling" sign (a lesion that looks different from all others on the patient).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6F66B679" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Diagnostic Procedure: If suspected, perform a full-thickness excisional biopsy with 2 mm margins (or a punch biopsy if the lesion is small enough).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="527324CF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Seborrheic Keratosis (SK)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="265805EC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Benign "old age barnacles". Variants include Dermatosis papulosa nigra and Acrochordons (skin tags).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="19E7F7B8" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Sign of Leser-Trélat: Sudden appearance of numerous SKs, or sudden increase in size of existing SKs. Strongly associated with internal malignancies, specifically gastrointestinal adenocarcinoma.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7316FE2B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5421BDE4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Presentations: Nodular (pearly with telangiectasias), Superficial, or Sclerosing (scar-like).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2D7EC0DA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Surgical Treatment: Excisional biopsy, electrodesiccation/curettage, or cryotherapy for small/superficial lesions.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="72B929AF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Mohs Micrographic Surgery Indications: Sclerosing type, poorly defined margins, high-recurrence anatomical areas (nose, eyelid), size &gt; 2 cm, or areas where tissue conservation is functionally critical.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="44810B25" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Medical Treatment: Imiquimod 5% cream or 5-Fluorouracil 5% for superficial lesions. Vismodegib (150 mg PO daily) is FDA-approved for advanced/metastatic BCC.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="25956BED" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) &amp; Precursors</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="705A22A2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Actinic Keratosis (AK): Precursor lesion on sun-exposed areas. Treated with topical 5-Fluorouracil (5% or 0.5%), Imiquimod 5%, Diclofenac 3%, Ingenol mebutate, or Tirbanibulin.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4CEFA241" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Bowen's Disease: Squamous cell carcinoma </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>in situ</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="203065C4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>SCC Features: Often arises on diseased/injured skin, or blossoms in patients undergoing immunosuppression (e.g., organ transplant recipients). Treated with surgical excision.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7BBCD4FC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Epidermal Cyst (Sebaceous Cyst)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1643BE03" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Benign nodule with a characteristic central punctum.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3FDD334F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="165" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Pathophysiology Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> The cyst wall produces </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>keratin</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> (cheesy-looking material), not sebum. They may spontaneously rupture and become inflamed.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="24063CB6" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><pict paralysis from acute stroke Seizure Disorders and Postictal States</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4980151B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="2E75B6" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="16" /><szCs val="16" /><color val="FFFFFF" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="16" /><szCs val="16" /><color val="FFFFFF" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  KEY REFERENCES</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="120" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="7F4F24" color="auto" /></pPr><r><rPr><b /><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="20" /><szCs val="20" /><color val="FFFFFF" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  AAFP Poll Questions — Session 10: Emergency Medicine I</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="0" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="7F4F24" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  6 practice questions from the AAFP Board Prep presentation. Work each question before checking the answer key.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  1.  A 14 y/o female arrives at the triage area of your local rural hospital Emergency Department with worsening abdominal pain for the past 2 days. The patient’s parents are out of town for the day and unavailable by cell phone. The patient is accompanied by her 17 y/o sister. The triage nurse asks for your advice. In this case, you would recommend:</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 10</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  2.  In this case, you would recommend: available physical exam until parental consent is available. with further care concern for acute pathology that could worsen if a delay were to occur EMTALA • The Emergency Medical Treatment and Labor Act (EMTALA) is a federal law that requires anyone coming to an emergency department to be stabilized and treated, regardless of their insurance status or ability to pay. Since its enactment in 1986 has remained an unfunded mandate. • If the hospital does not have the capability to treat the emergency medical condition, an "appropriate" transfer of the patient to another hospital must be done in accordance with the EMTALA provisions. • Hospitals with specialized capabilities are obligated to accept transfers from hospitals who lack the capability to treat unstable emergency medial conditions. What are the requirements for transferring patients under EMTALA?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Do not perform a H&amp;P on the patient until parental consent is</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Obtain a history from the patient, but do not perform a</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Inquire if the patient could be pregnant, and if so, proceed</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Perform an H&amp;P and proceed with diagnostic testing if there is</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 10</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  3.  You are on a plane ride when a passenger suddenly becomes unresponsive. Which statement below is true?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Low-risk  ASA</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Increased risk  anticoagulate 2 Prior Stroke or TIA</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 10</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  4.  A 65 y/o non-smoker with history of controlled HTN and hyperlipidemia, presents for a routine exam. According to USPSTF, which of the following should be performed to assess his risk of stroke?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  No additional testing for stroke risk</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Auscultate for carotid bruits</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Obtain carotid duplex ultrasound</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Obtain CT-Angiography of the carotid arteries</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 10</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  5.  An 82 y/o patient developed sudden dysarthria and RUE weakness. Onset at 8am. He/she arrives at the ED at 11 am. IV is inserted, labs sent. Which imaging study should be ordered STAT?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Non-contrast head CT</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Contrast head CT</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  CT- A (angiogram) of the head</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Non-contrast MRI of head</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 10</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  6.  An 82 yo patient developed sudden dysarthria and RUE weakness. Onset at 8am. He/she arrives at the ED at 11 am. BP= 200/100; At 12 noon, the labs and head CT are reported as “normal.” The patient is NOT a candidate for thrombolytic therapy Your management will include….?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  20 prevention of CVA and TIA: diagnostic evaluation</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  20 prevention of CVA and TIA: lifestyle changes</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  20 prevention of CVA and TIA: antiplatelet Rx</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Carotid endarterectomy</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 10</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="40" after="120" /><shd val="clear" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><pBdr><bottom val="single" sz="4" color="1F3864" /></pBdr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="1F3864" /><spacing before="0" rsidRDefault="000B00BD"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFFFFF" /><spacing before="60" after="60" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><color val="000000" /><sz val="6" /><szCs /></pPr><r><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>10. EMERGENCY MEDICINE: GENERAL PRINCIPLES &amp; RESUSCITATION</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="441631B1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>A. Legal &amp; Administrative Principles</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="227D3409" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="162" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>EMTALA (Emergency Medical Treatment and Labor Act)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="384203DA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="162" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Enacted in 1986 as the "anti-dumping" rule. An unfunded federal mandate requiring that anyone coming to an ED be stabilized and treated, regardless of insurance status or ability to pay.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="579AA014" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="162" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Treating Minors:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Minors may request emergency evaluation. Do </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> delay a history and physical exam or diagnostic testing for parental consent if there is concern for acute pathology that could worsen with delay.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="61FC0FF5" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="162" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Good Samaritan Laws</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1DFD0B0A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="162" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Protects providers in all 50 states and on airlines (Aviation Medical Assistance Act of 1998).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0F9D3ABB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="162" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>General Principles: There is no legal obligation to provide aid. Provides immunity from malpractice unless there is gross, willful/wanton negligence, or lack of "good faith."</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1DE2BEF9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="162" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Administrative Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Legal immunity is immediately withdrawn if the physician accepts payment or sends a bill for the aid rendered. (Accepting a gift is permitted).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1EC52249" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>B. Resuscitation (BLS &amp; ACLS)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0BC11D78" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="226" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Basic Life Support Rules</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0A943AFF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="226" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Sequence is "CAB" (Compressions, Airway, Breathing).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="73A7F72A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="226" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Pulse Check Nuance:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> The general public should </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> perform a pulse check. Healthcare providers should perform a pulse check for &lt; 10 seconds. If no pulse is present, immediately start chest compressions.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0DC1FEBF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="226" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>"Shockable" Rhythms (VFib / Pulseless V-Tach)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="66569BDC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="226" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Administer Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="23A622C9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="226" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Defibrillate. If the rhythm persists, administer Amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="37914705" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="226" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>"Nonshockable" Rhythms (Asystole / PEA)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5B5E6807" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="226" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Continue CPR for 2 minutes. Administer Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4FC9ACE2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="226" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Board Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Atropine is no longer recommended or utilized for Asystole/PEA.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="06FA39DD" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="226" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Evaluate for the 5 Ts (Toxins, Tamponade, Tension pneumo, Thrombosis-cardiac, Thrombosis-PE) and 6 Hs (H+/acidosis, Hypothermia, Hypokalemia, Hyperkalemia, Hypovolemia, Hypoxia).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="488A8C01" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="226" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Defibrillation vs. Synchronized Cardioversion</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0629EF76" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="226" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Synchronized Cardioversion:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Indicated for unstable Narrow Regular (SVT/A-Flutter at 50-100 J), Narrow Irregular (A-Fib at 120-200 J), and Wide Regular (Monomorphic V-Tach at 100 J).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="11D44754" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="226" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Defibrillation (Unsynchronized):</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Strictly for Wide Irregular (Polymorphic V-Tach) and Ventricular Fibrillation.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="17C1239B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>C. Sepsis Management</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="677FACBA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="99" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Definitions (Sepsis-3 Guidelines)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="281236FC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="99" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Sepsis:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> A life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. (Note: SIRS criteria are no longer part of the official definition, and scoring systems like SOFA/qSOFA are predictive of mortality, not specifically diagnostic).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5DD42C19" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="99" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Septic Shock:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Sepsis + circulatory, cellular, and metabolic dysfunction associated with a higher risk of mortality.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2FACB105" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="99" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Treatment Protocol (Surviving Sepsis Campaign)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0F0FE2EA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="99" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Measure lactate and follow levels every 4-6 hours.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6F80D32B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Obtain blood cultures </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>prior</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> to the administration of antibiotics.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3C32003F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="99" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour of recognition.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="654052D1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="99" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Resuscitate with a minimum of 30 mL/kg of IV crystalloid fluid within the first 3 hours.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6FB60546" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="99" /></numPr><spacing after="220" xml:space="preserve"> If the patient remains hypotensive (MAP &lt; 65) after the fluid challenge, the vasopressor of choice is Norepinephrine.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="34F5371B" ns2:textId="77777777"
