Child abuse should be suspected in all of the following EXCEPT: hemorrhages Child Abuse: Wide spectrum of injuries Shaken Baby syndrome: Diffuse cerebral injury with edema, +/- intracerebral bleed, retinal hemorrhages 3 Pearls--- 1. “If they don’t cruise, they don’t bruise” 2. Rib fractures - &lt; age 3, 82% are abuse 62 children, Univ. Colorado, Barsness, KA,et al. J Trauma 2003 3. Undiagnosed==&gt; &gt; 25% mortality in 2 yrs Toddler’s Fracture – Is Not “Abuse” • Most common fx in age 9 mos – 3 yrs that present with a limp (29 of 100) • Spiral fracture of distal tibia • Best seen on oblique view • May be occult • Below knee walking cast x 3 weeks Question 2: Wound care and soft tissue extremity injury • A 27 yo tripped and fell in the garden sustaining a laceration to the thenar eminence of the hand. The patient noted there were glass slivers in the ground and is uncertain if there is glass remaining in the wound. The wound is a linear 3cm laceration, without active bleeding. Despite the patient’s efforts to clean the wound, there appears to be dirt in the wound. The patient’s last tetanus immunization was 7 years ago. Question 2: All of the following would be appropriate except: Wound Care: Tetanus • Clostridium tetani – anaerobic Gr. (+), ubiquitous, soil • 233 cases in US from 2001-2008 (approx 30 cases/yr) • Elderly, immigrants • Clean wound &gt; 10 years since last dose • Dirty wound &gt; 5 years since last dose • Safe in pregnancy Substitute Tdap for Td once, then Td booster (or Tdap) every 10 years Can I Irrigate With Tap Water?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Classic: elderly, develops gradually</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Exertional f. Skin disease</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Provide a tetanus booster</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Obesity</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 11</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  2.  Abdominal wall and hernias A 72 yo female presents with a 6-hour hx of a painful lump in her groin. Some nausea, no vomiting. No fever/chills. Exam: Abdomen: soft, non-tender, R groin with palpable mass below (inferior to) inguinal ligament. Despite gentle, firm pressure, the mass does not disappear.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 11</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  3.  The most likely diagnosis is: Abdominal Wall and Hernias Epidemiology: • 1 million hernia repairs are performed each year in the US • Inguinal hernia repairs constituting &gt; 3/4 of these cases • Approximately 90% of all inguinal hernia repairs are performed on men Terminology: – Reducible: – Incarcerated: i.e., Stuck!”(despite gentle firm pressure) – Strangulated: vascular compromise (surgical emergency) Abdominal Wall Hernias: Types •Indirect inguinal hernia: 75% of all hernias!!! -Most common hernia in both sexes!!! -Men are 5x more common -More likely to incarcerate/strangulate than direct •Direct inguinal hernia: 2nd most common Note: You can NOT differentiate between direct and indirect hernias on exam!!! •Femoral hernias: more common in women - Located below inguinal ligament, medial to femoral pulse Groin Hernia – is watchful waiting appropriate?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Strangulated indirect inguinal hernia</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Reducible direct inguinal hernia</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Incarcerated femoral hernia</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Incarcerated Spigelian hernia</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 11</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  4.  A 67 yo patient with a history chronic LBP presents with 6 hours of sudden, severe diffuse abdominal pain. Medications: celecoxib (Celebrex) chronic use- - methylprednisolone (Medrol dose-pak) added 3 days ago Vital Signs: BP=140/90, P=105, RR=30, afebrile. His abdomen is diffusely tender, with guarding and rebound. An upright Chest x-ray demonstrates free air under the diaphragm</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 11</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  5.  The most likely diagnosis is: Pneumoperitoneum: Etiology • Perforated duodenal ulcer – The most common cause Especially of the anterior aspect of the first part of the duodenum • Perforated peptic ulcer • Bowel obstruction • Ruptured diverticulum • Penetrating trauma • Ruptured inflammatory bowel disease (eg, megacolon) • Necrotizing enterocolitis • Cancer • Ischemic bowel • After laparotomy; after laparoscopy An Upright CXR is 80% Sensitive for Free Air Can detect as little as 1- 2 mL of gas under the diaphragm or lateral margin of the liver Courtesy: Wikimedia -https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File%3APneumoperitoneum_modification.jpg</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Acute ischemic bowel</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Perforated duodenal ulcer</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Perforated appendicitis</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Perforated diverticulitis</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 11</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  6.  A 60 yo patient presents to the ED/office with 3 days of worsening RUQ pain. The pain radiates to the right scapula. (+) nausea and vomiting. Temp=39 C, (102.2 F), P=122, BP= 90/45 Appearance is jaundiced (total bilirubin = 7.8), RUQ is tender.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 11</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  7.  The most likely diagnosis is: Gallstone Disease: Who Is at Risk?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Acute cholecystitis</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Ascending cholangitis</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Gallstone pancreatitis</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Gallstone ileus</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 11</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  8.  You are evaluating a 6 y/o for abdominal pain. You have a moderate degree of suspicion that the patient may have appendicitis. The American College of Radiology (ACR) and American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) recommends which of the following as the first choice to evaluate this patient?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Abdominal x-ray</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Abdominal ultrasound</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  CT scan of abdomen and pelvis</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  MRI of abdomen and pelvis</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 11</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  9.  Intestinal Obstruction The most common cause of large bowel obstruction is: Intestinal Obstruction Small bowel (85%) Large bowel (15%) • Adhesions from previous • Neoplasm (60%) surgery (60%) • Diverticulitis (20%) • Malignant tumor (20%) • Volvulus (10-15%) • Herniation (10%) • Intussusception (&lt; 5%) • Inflammatory bowel • Miscellaneous disease (5%) • Volvulus (3%) • Misc (2%) Small Bowel Obstruction • Colicky abdominal pain •Distention • Nausea and vomiting •Tympany, high-pitch BS • Diarrhea (early finding) •Lack of flatus and constipation •Fever/tachycardia (with strangulation) •Abdominal tenderness and peritoneal signs (with strangulation) Small Bowel Obstruction: Radiography • Dilated loops of bowel ------------------&gt; • Paucity of air in colon • Air fluid levels Jackson PG, et al. AFP 2011 From: Wikipedia Small Bowel Obstruction: Management • NGT, IV hydration, analgesia • Nonoperative trial up to 3 days is warranted. Successful 40-70% • B. If unstable/peritonitis or (+) suggestion of strangulation…. Go to surgery!!!! Large Bowel Obstruction Symptoms • More common in elderly • Crampy abdominal pain – Sudden onset: think acute obstruction (eg, volvulus) • Nausea, vomiting, distension Signs • Hypertympanic to percussion • Bowel sounds normal early, then become quiet • Fever/tenderness/rigidity – is a bad sign • Cecum is region most likely to perforate Large Bowel Obstruction - A “closed loop” obstruction (with a competent ileocecal valve) - Cecum preferentially dilates - Dilation &gt; 12-14 cm increases risk of perforation Malignant Bowel Obstruction #1: Surgical #2: End-of-life/Palliative care • Add Dexamethasone 6-16mg /day. NNT = 61,2 1 Feuer DJ, et al Cochrane Review 2000 2Albert RH. End-of-Life Care: Managing Common Symptoms. Am Fam Physician 2017 Sigmoid Volvulus A long mesentery with a narrow base of fixation to the retroperitoneum and elongated, redundant bowel predisposes to the formation of volvulus Sigmoid colon most common Result of chronic constipation At-risk patients: •Neuropsychiatric disorders •Institutionalized/NH patients •Parkinson disease Image reprinted with permission from Medscape Reference(http://emedicine.medscape •Multiple sclerosis .com/), 2014, available at: http://emedicine •Spinal cord injury .medscape.com/article/2048554-overview. •Excessive laxatives, enemas Sigmoid Volvulus Average age: 8th decade Treatment: endoscopic decompression - then semi-elective surgery - if no surgery: up to 50% will have recurrence 12-15% mortality rate Image reprinted with permission from Medscape Reference (http://emedicine.medscape.com/), 2014, available at: http://emedicine.medscape.com/article/2048554-overview. Cecal Volvulus Hereditary condition: congenital incomplete dorsal mesenteric fixation of the cecum or ascending colon associated with an abnormally elongated mesentery distal to this area of absent mesentery Women &gt; men Most common: 6th decade of life Treatment: Surgery Image reprinted with permission from Medscape Reference Right hemicolectomy (http://emedicine.medscape.com/), 2014, available at: http://emedicine.medscape.com/article/774045-overview. Vascular disease and the Abdomen: Chronic mesenteric ischemia (“intestinal angina”) Acute mesenteric ischemia “pain out of proportion to finding” Ischemic colitis “Low-flow state” SMA Acute mesenteric venous IMA thrombosis “DVT of intestinal vessels” Vascular disease and the Abdomen: Chronic mesenteric ischemia (“intestinal angina”) Acute mesenteric ischemia “pain out of proportion to finding” Ischemic colitis “Low-flow state” SMA Acute mesenteric venous IMA thrombosis “DVT of intestinal vessels” Vascular disease and the Abdomen: Chronic mesenteric ischemia (“intestinal angina”) Acute mesenteric ischemia “pain out of proportion to finding” Ischemic colitis SMA “Low-flow state” Acute mesenteric venous IMA thrombosis “DVT of intestinal vessels” Vascular disease and the Abdomen: Chronic mesenteric ischemia (“intestinal angina”) Acute mesenteric ischemia “pain out of proportion to finding” Ischemic colitis SMA “Low-flow state” Acute mesenteric venous IMA thrombosis “DVT of intestinal vessels” Pediatric Surgery See supplemental slides for more details Thank you! Answers 1.D 2.B 3.C 4.B 5.B 6.B 7.B Supplemental Slides Head Injury- Prevention: Helmets • Motorcycle helmets: decrease head injury (69%) and death (42%) • Cochrane Library 2008, Issue 3 • Bicycle helmets decrease head injury (63%), brain injury (88%) and facial injuries (65%) • Cochrane Library 2008, Issue 3 • Skiing helmets decrease head injury (60%) • Sulheim, S. et al. JAMA 295:919, Feb 22, 2006 Eye Emergencies: • Corneal abrasion: Level of evidence • Topical NSAID’s offer effective pain relief from B and may result in earlier return to work. • Topical cycloplegics and mydriatics do not relieve pain B and are not recommended. • Eye patch does not improve pain and can delay healing A • Topical antibiotics may be prescribed to prevent C bacterial superinfection in corneal abrasions. If associated with contact lens – a) Do NOT reinsert lens b) use antipseudomonalantibiotic Eye Emergencies: A. Trauma-related "I got punched in the eye” • Orbital fracture: • Check EOM Most common= restricted upward gaze • Anesthesia distribution infraorbital nerve • Traumatic iritis: • Inflammation of iris and ciliary muscle • Provide Analgesia Mydriatics alleviate the pain associated with ciliary spasm (light sensitivity) • Hyphema: blood in anterior chamber • Elevate head of bed 30 degrees at rest • Avoid ASA/NSAID’s, ophtho consult Eye Emergencies: Chemical Burns: Alkali worse than acid • • Irrigate, irrigate, irrigate (2L) Radiation keratitis • Flourescein demonstrates multiple punctate lesions • Pain relief!!! • Encourage prevention! Eye Emergencies: Subconjunctival hemorrhage It’s not an emergency!! DO NOTHING!!!!!! (Reassure) Avoid straining The Eye Emergencies Painless Painful • Direct blow • Foreign body Does proparacaine relieve pain?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" Classic: elderly, develops gradually Check Labs, admit</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC)</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 11</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="40" after="120" /><shd val="clear" fill="7F4F24" xml:space="preserve" /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="61473118" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFFFFF" /><spacing before="60" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00586D48" rsidP="00586D48"><pPr><pStyle val="Style1" /></pPr><r><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>11. NEUROLOGIC EMERGENCIES: STROKE &amp; TIA</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="34355AFD" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>A. Stroke Subtypes &amp; Risk Factors</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7DF0724D" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="19" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Epidemiology</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0C5DDE60" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="19" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Ischemic (88%):</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Thrombotic (50-58%, typically large vessel), Embolic (30-38%, cardiogenic emboli typically cause large strokes).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0828B9A8" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="19" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Hemorrhagic (12%):</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Intracerebral (10%), Subarachnoid (2%).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1D671F06" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="19" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Risk Factors:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Hypertension, Diabetes, and Smoking account for 90% of all strokes.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="181DFD7A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="19" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Primary Prevention Nuances</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5191F20F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="19" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Aspirin:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> The 2022 USPSTF guidelines recommend </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>against</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> initiating low-dose aspirin for primary prevention in adults  &gt; 60 (Grade D recommendation). For adults 40-59 with  &gt; 10% 10-year CVD risk, the net benefit is small and the decision should be individualized (Grade C).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="670000E3" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="19" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Carotid Screening:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> USPSTF recommends </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>against</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> screening asymptomatic patients for carotid artery stenosis (Grade D). </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Clinical Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> This recommendation strictly advises against ordering carotid duplex ultrasounds AND against routine auscultation for carotid bruits.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="062F03B2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="19" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (SAH)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="54AD8779" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="19" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Presents as a sudden "thunderclap" headache ("worst headache of my life") with nuchal rigidity.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2033158F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="19" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Epidemiology Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> 80% are due to saccular (berry) aneurysms. SAH occurs almost exclusively in middle-aged or older adults (peak age 50). Do </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> aggressively work up a 17-year-old for an SAH.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="62CED84D" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="19" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Pathophysiology Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Approximately 2% of the US population harbors a brain aneurysm that will never rupture.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="344CFB82" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>B. Acute Stroke Management</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="08597589" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="281" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Initial Imaging</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6AFEAAEF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="281" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Order a STAT Non-contrast head CT. The sole immediate goal is to differentiate ischemic stroke (dry) from hemorrhagic stroke (white blood present in the brain).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="46F08C6F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="281" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Blood Pressure Control</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="154850AC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="281" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Ischemic Stroke:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Do NOT acutely lower blood pressure. Elevated BP is the body's natural physiologic response to maintain cerebral perfusion to the threatened "penumbra." Only treat if BP is &gt; 220/120 mmHg. If over this threshold, cautiously lower by 15% using Labetalol (10 mg q10-20 min) or Nicardipine (5 mg/hr, titrate q5 min).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="124BA1DE" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="281" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Hemorrhagic Stroke:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Lower systolic BP to &lt; 140 mmHg (Class I, Level A).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7A8D6AC8" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="281" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Subarachnoid Hemorrhage:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Lower systolic BP to &lt; 160 mmHg (Class IIa, Level C).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1E467025" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="281" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Thrombolytic Therapy (tPA/Alteplase) Criteria</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0F490E17" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="281" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Age &lt; 80:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Onset of symptoms &lt; 4.5 hours.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="00AE493F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="281" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Age &gt; 80:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Onset of symptoms &lt; 3.0 hours. No anticoagulants allowed.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="393CB4E7" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="281" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>Universal Requirements:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> CT reveals no hemorrhage, no history of intracerebral hemorrhage, no seizure at onset, and BP &lt; 185/110.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="68F4D28A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="281" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Acute Pharmacology to AVOID</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="133DEE86" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="281" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Strictly avoid initiating prophylactic antiseizure medications, IV Heparin, or Warfarin.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="49F43DDB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="281" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Do</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> initiate Aspirin.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="39F5D6B9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>C. Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA) &amp; Secondary Prevention</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="273B3AB8" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="191" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>The Modern Definition of TIA</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="26487192" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="191" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>The classic "24-hour" and "1-hour" time-limit definitions are obsolete.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4439C7E1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="191" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">TIA is now a </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>tissue-based definition:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> A transient episode of neurologic dysfunction caused by focal ischemia </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>without evidence of acute infarction</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> on imaging.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="77B5D468" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="191" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Diagnostic Standard:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Requires an MRI with Diffusion-Weighted Imaging (DWI) showing no white cytotoxic edema/cell death.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="15A29989" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="191" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Mimic Clue:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Anterior circulation TIAs/strokes present with a </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>loss</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> of function (loss of strength, speech, vision). If the patient experiences a </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>gain</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> of function (e.g., tingling), suspect an alternative pathology. </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Syncope (loss of consciousness) is global hypoperfusion, not a TIA.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="19E8EB12" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="191" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Secondary Pharmacologic Prevention (Antiplatelets)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="178EE443" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="191" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Driven by the CHANCE and POINT trials.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5FD7A632" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="191" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>If a patient presents with a Minor Stroke (NIHSS  &lt; 3) or a High-Risk TIA (ABCD2 Score  &gt; 4), immediately start Dual Antiplatelet Therapy (DAPT: Aspirin 50-325 mg + Clopidogrel 75 mg).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6F0FAD6B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="191" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>DAPT should be continued for 21 to 90 days, followed by a transition back to monotherapy.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="477CD93B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="191" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Clinical Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> If a patient fails monotherapy and has a recurrent TIA, there is no evidence-based guideline to support restarting DAPT or increasing dosages, though it is commonly attempted in practice.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1BA693E9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="191" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Surgical Prevention</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="60852590" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="191" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA) is recommended for patients with a non-disabling stroke/TIA and ipsilateral 70-99% stenosis (1A recommendation) or 50-69% stenosis (1B recommendation).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6EFEEFEF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00586D48" rsidRDefault="00586D48" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr></p><p ns2:paraId="45478FDF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00586D48" rsidRDefault="00586D48" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr></p><p ns2:paraId="65673527" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00586D48" rsidRDefault="00586D48" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr></p><p ns2:paraId="436EFC86" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing Which of the following bites has the highest risk of infection?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Cat Bite</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Dog Bite</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" bite to the arm</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" male presents with a 3-day history of a rash that is increasing in size. • It is not (-) painful or tender. • It is flat, oval, 14 cm x 7 cm in size, has central clearing and has no fluctuance. • No In this case, you would: Western blot study and if positive treat with ceftriaxone. Lyme Disease • Due to spirochete: Borrelia burgdorferi • Transmitted by: Deer tick – Ixodes scapularis and Ixodes pacificus Courtesy of CDC Courtesy of the CDC Lyme Disease: Stages • Early Lyme Disease –Erythema migrans: present 50-70% of cases Courtesy of Wikipedia Courtesy of the CDC/James Gathany Lyme Disease: Stages • Early Lyme Disease –Erythema migrans: present 50-70% of cases –Neurologic disease: Cranial neuropathy (7th nerve), radiculopathy, lymphocytic meningitis –Cardiac disease: think A-V block Lyme Disease: Stages • Late Lyme disease –Lyme arthritis: •Large joints, typically knees –Neurologic disease: •Encephalopathy •Peripheral neuropathy • Post-Lyme disease syndromes – “…unexplained chronic subjective symptoms following treatment…” IDSA guideline, 2006 Lyme Disease: Treatment Stage 1: Do not test, just treat Adults • Doxycycline 100 mg BID x 10 days • Amoxicillin 500 mg TID x 14 days • Cefuroxime 500 mg BID x 14 days • Azithromycin 500 mg qd x 7 days IDSA guideline, CID Jan 23, 2021 Lyme Disease: Treatment Stage 1: Do not test, just treat Children • Doxycycline: &gt; age 8, 2 mg/kg BID • Amoxicillin: 50 mg/kg/d divided TID • Cefuroxime 30 mg/kg/d divided BID • Azithromycin 10 mg/kg/d qd Medical Letter, May 9, 2016 Photo courtesy of CDC Lyme Disease: Testing •Stage 1: Do not test, just treat So who do you test?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Prescribe ceftriaxone 1gm IM/IV</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Prescribe doxycycline 100 mg BID x 10 days</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Obtain a Lyme titer; if positive treat with ceftriaxone.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Obtain a Lyme titer; if positive, confirm with a</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 12</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  4.  • A 64 yo male presents to the ED with diffuse pruritus and erythema along with facial and oral swelling. • This occurred 15 minutes after eating peanuts. • His blood pressure is 65/35 mm Hg, pulse is 120 bpm. See photos - next slides</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 12</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  5.  The first medication this patient should receive is: Allergy: Reactions • Urticaria (hives) - IgE mediated • Angioedema - may be 1. IgE-mediated or 2. Idiopathic (ACE-induced), not true allergy Result: Swelling of face, neck, and tongue • Anaphylaxis - may occur within seconds to 1 hour – Skin rash, respiratory symptoms, hypotension, GI distress • Anaphylactoid reactions: Non-immunologic (non-IgE) - direct release of granules from mast cells and basophils - not true allergy (Example: radiocontrast) Allergic Reactions: Etiologic Agents Anaphylactic: Anaphylactoid: IgE-dependent Non-IgE/nonimmunologic • Food (35%) • Opioids • Medication (20%) • ASA and NSAIDs • Insect venom (20%) IgE • Radiocontrast media Y • Latex • Exercise Allergic Reactions: Etiologic Agents Anaphylactic: Anaphylactoid: IgE-dependent Non-IgE/nonimmunologic • Food • Opioids • Medication • ASA and NSAIDs • Insect venom • Radiocontrast media • Latex • Exercise Allergic Reaction: Treatment • Vasoconstrictors: Epinephrine 0.3-0.5 cc 1:1000 solution, SQ or IM 1-5 cc of 1:10,000 solution, IV 1 ug/min (1:10,000) 1 mg in 1L NS at 1 ml/min Allergic Reaction: Treatment • Vasoconstrictors: Epinephrine If Epi-Pen is used, refer to ED for follow up Reason: chance of biphasic reaction “second wave” Biphasic reaction occurs in 0.4-20% of patients However, clinically severe reactions are approx. 0.2% Allergic Reactions: Treatment • Vasoconstrictors: Epinephrine *Mild-moderate: 0.3-0.5 cc 1:1000 solution, SQ or IM **Severe: 1-5 cc of 1:10,000 solution, IV ***If pt. on B-Blocker, give glucagon 1-5 mg IV • H1 antagonist: 1st or 2nd generation antihistamines • H2 antagonist: H2-blocker du jour decreases itch • Steroids: - Do nothing for acute episode - Do not prevent biphasic reaction* This is NOT an allergic reaction….. ACE-Induced Angioedema • NOT an allergic reaction • Due to accumulation of bradykinin (?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Mild-moderate:</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Severe:</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Methylprednisolone</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Ranitidine</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 12</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  6.  Toxicology A 21 yo college student presents to the ED with friends who report the student swallowed “a whole bottle” of acetaminophen 45 minutes before arrival. They also note the patient has been drinking alcohol. The patient is awake but appears intoxicated.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 12</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  7.  Which of the following would be the best course of action: Toxicology: Gastric Decontamination • Syrup of ipecac - No, No, No!!!!!!! -AAP says do not keep in home (Pediatrics, Nov 2003) • Gastric emptying - 36-40 Fr tube - Possibly helpful if used within 60 min - Risk for iatrogenic injury (aspiration, esophagus) • Charcoal (best option) - If given &lt; 30 min, decreases absorption by 70% - If given 30-60 min, decreases absorption by 30% - Dose: 1-2 g/kg (max 100 g) Acetaminophen Toxicity • Max daily dosing: 4 g per day, toxic dose = 150 mg/kg • 2nd most common cause of liver transplantation in US, however, only 4% of those with hepatoxicity develop liver failure • 4 clinical phases: - Phase 1 (0-24 hrs): asymptomatic, nausea/vomiting - Phase 2 (18-72 hrs): RUQ abd pain, N/V, rising LFTs - Phase 3 (72-96 hrs): Abd pain, N/V, jaundice, encephalopathy, renal failure, death - Phase 4 (4 -14 days): resolution • Acetaminophen levels: drawn at 4 hours after ingestion, treatment based on Rumack-Matthew nomogram • Treatment: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) Toxicology Emergencies Agent Antidote •Acetaminophen……………..•N-Acetylcysteine •Aspirin………………………..•Alkaline diuresis •B-blocker…………………….•Glucagon •Ca-channel blocker…………•Glucagon •Digitalis………………………•Fab antibodies •Heparin………………………•Protamine Sulfate •Isoniazid (INH)………………•Pyridoxine (Vit B6) •Opiates……………………….•Naloxone (Narcan) •Organophosphates…………•.Atropine •TCA…………………………..•NaHCO3 Common Toxidromes #1. Anticholinergic • Presentation: – Hot as Hades……..Hyperthermia – Blind as a Bat……..Mydriasis – Dry as a Bone…….Thirst, decreased salivation – Red as a Beet……..Flushing, vasodilation – Mad as a Hatter…..Delirium, agitation, confusion • Etiology: Antihistamines, Antiparkinson, Antipsychotics, Antiemetics (phenothiazines), Antidepressants (TCA), Antispasmotics Common Toxidromes #2. Serotonin Syndrome • Presentation: – Cognitive-behavior: agitation, anxiety, drowsy, delirium, headache, seizures – Autonomic dysfunction: tachycardia, arrhythmias, hyperthermia, HTN, diaphoresis, diarrhea, nausea – Neuromuscular: restlessness, tremor, hyperreflexia, clonus, dysarthria, ataxia, – Etiology: – Most common: SSRIs, MAOs – Especially if combined with: meperidine, cocaine, dextromethorphan, venlafaxine, amphetamine Watch out for: tramadol, linezolid (Zyvox) Serotonin Syndrome: Presentation Boyer E and Shannon M. N EnglJ Med 2005;352:1112-1120. Image © New England Journal of Medicine. Differences Between Serotonin and Anticholinergic Syndromes Skin Muscular Tone Reflexes Serotonin syndrome Diaphoretic Increased Hyperreflexia Anticholinergic Dry Normal Normal Neuroleptic malignant Diaphoretic, “Lead pipe” rigid Bradyreflexia syndrome pallor Toxicology Treatment for the serotonin syndrome is: Cyproheptadine - Has antiserotonergic properties - Only available orally Thank you! C Supplemental slides Lyme Disease: Other types of testing Just say “No” • Examples of unvalidated tests include: • Capture assays for antigens in urine • Culture, immunofluorescence staining, or cell sorting of cell wall-deficient or cystic forms of B. burgdorferi • Lymphocyte transformation tests • Quantitative CD57 lymphocyte assays • Reverse Western blots • In-house criteria for interpretation of immunoblots • Measurements of antibodies in joint fluid (synovial fluid) • IgM or IgG tests without a previous ELISA/EIA/IFA MMWR, 2014;63:333 Johnson BJ, PilgardMA, Russell TM.J ClinMicrobiol2014;52:721-4. MMWR, CDC Surveillance Summary, 2005;54:125. Marques A, Brown MR, Fleisher TA:.ClinVaccine Immunol2009, 16:1249-1250. Other Tick-Borne Illnesses • Tularemia • Bartonella • Q fever • Relapsing fever • STARI-Masters’ disease • Colorado tick fever • Tick paralysis • Borrelia mayonii • African tick bite fever • Ehrlichiosis Allergic Reactions: Treatment H2 blockers help decrease urticaria Methods: 91 ED pts. with acute allergic symptoms, randomized to: Results: Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV + ranitidine 50 mg IV + placebo Urticaria at 2 hrs 8% 26% Need for additional antihistamines 4% 23% Lin, RY, et al. Ann Emerg Med 36(5): 462, Nov. 2000. xml:space="preserve" /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="8EB25582" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFFFFF" /><spacing before="60"
