You are called to admit an 85 y.o. man for UTI by the ED. His daughter is present and asks about placement to a skilled nursing facility. To qualify for SNF the patient must meet which criteria: Case Discussion An 80 yo man is referred for evaluation of possible depression. He’s accompanied by his wife who describes how much more difficulty ambulating he’s had since esophagectomy for cancer 18 months ago. His medications include hydrochlorothiazide, lisinopril, risperidone, metoclopramide and valproate. Case Discussion On exam, the patient has a flat affect and blinks little. He slowly rocks back and forth in his chair when asked to stand but is unable to propel himself to a standing position. When helped up to a standing position, he has trouble initiating his gait, then takes a few small steps and freezes.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Inpatient status and admitted for at least 2 nights</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  By being admitted to the hospital, he qualifies.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Observation status and admitted for at least 3 nights</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Inpatient status and admitted for at least 3 nights</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 18</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  3.  Your next step is: Pearls • Drug-induced parkinsonism can occur with medications overlooked as considered culprits (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, sodium valproate). • Atypical antipsychotics may cause parkinsonism: risperidone, olanzapine, et al. • Quetiapine, pimavanserin, clozapine have little extrapyramidal toxicity Pearls • Typical Symptoms of Parkinson Disease • Asymmetric Resting Tremor • Bradykinesia • Rigidity • Postural Instability (late) • Hypophonia • Loss of smell • Micrographia PD Treatment • Response to levodopa can be diagnostic • Carbidopa/Levodopa 25mg/100mg • Start low and increase every 1-2 weeks • Can take up to 2 months (sometimes immediate) Carbidopa/Levodopa (Sinemet) • Most effective med for gait (bradykinesia, rigidity); tremor response variable. • Carbidopa prevents peripheral breakdown of levodopa; &gt;75 mg daily for max effect. • Begin 25/100 bid or tid; may use 25/250 as dose increased; avg patient needs about 500 to 1,000 mg L-dopa/day. Treating PD in the Elderly • Carbidopa/levodopa is the most effective medication for PD; optimize dose before adding other drugs • Anticholinergics and amantadine have little role in treating elderly PD patients. • COMT inhibitors and MAO inhibitors are very expensive for modest gain. • Dopamine agonist more likely to cause delusions/ hallucinations</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Begin carbidopa/levodopa 25 mg/100 mg tid</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Stop the risperidone, metoclopramide and valproate</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Begin amantadine 100 mg bid</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" lisinopril</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs [See session slides for full context]</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Age-related macular degeneration</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Cataracts</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Glaucoma</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Diabetic retinopathy</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 18</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  8.  The most appropriate next step is: Charles Bonnet Syndrome Experienced by 10-13% of patients with severe vision loss Visual (not auditory) hallucinations Similar to a phantom limb syndrome (for the eyes)</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Send a urinalysis and urine culture</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Reassurance and education</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Start a second generation antipsychotic</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Non urgent head imaging</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 18</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  9.  Presbycusis is typically associated with the reduction in what type of hearing loss?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Hearing threshold at all frequencies</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Hearing threshold at higher frequencies</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Hearing threshold at lower frequencies</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Hearing threshold at midrange frequencies</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 18</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  10.  What rheumatological condition associated with this patient’s primary diagnosis is she at also risk of developing?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00586D48" rsidP="00586D48"><pPr><pStyle val="Style1" /></pPr><r><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>19. GERIATRICS: SYSTEMS OF CARE &amp; COMMON SYNDROMES</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7A586259" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>A. Health Care Policy &amp; Medicare Coverage</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2BC1F555" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="241" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Medicare Part A (Inpatient &amp; Facility Care)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4AED813D" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="241" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Skilled Nursing Facility (SNF) / Short-Stay Rehab:</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1483E651" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="241" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Coverage Rules:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Days 1-20 are typically fully covered. Days 21-100 require a 20% copay (often picked up by secondary insurance).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="23276D25" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="241" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Therapy Requirement:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Must require at least 5 days of therapy per week. Medicare pays a per diem to the facility covering therapy, meds, food, and nursing care.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3A92F55D" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="241" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>The "3-Midnight" SNF Qualification Rule:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> To qualify for SNF placement, the patient must have "Inpatient" status and be admitted for at least 3 nights. </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Administrative Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> The day of discharge does </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> count as one of the three days, effectively requiring 3 midnights. Observation status does </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> qualify.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="32DBD138" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="241" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Hospice: Requires a life expectancy of  &lt; 6 months. Paid via a per diem. </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Hospice provides bereavement services for 11 months after death, which has been shown to lower depression rates in family members.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="35F6226B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="241" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Home Health &amp; Hospital: Also covered under Part A.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3E932AE1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="241" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Medicare Parts B, C, and D</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2EE0C292" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="241" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Part B: Covers outpatient services, ambulance, and Durable Medical Equipment (DME).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7F9523CA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="241" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Part C (Medicare Advantage/Managed Care): Includes Part A and B services. </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Traditional Medicare does not cover vision or dental, but Part C plans often do, and may include gym memberships (e.g., Silver Sneakers). </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Counseling Tip:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Advise turning-65 patients to purchase the most comprehensive plan they can afford initially, as it is much harder to increase the level of coverage later due to extra steps/requirements.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="178B0AE6" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="241" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Part D: Covers prescription drugs.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4C2DDF9D" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="241" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Medicaid &amp; Long-Term Care</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="16E85D82" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="241" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Medicare does </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> pay for long-term residential care or assisted living.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6B58050A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="241" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Medicaid is typically the only health insurance that pays for residential care costs.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2AC62F0B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="241" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>It varies state-to-state, is based on modified adjusted gross income, and applies to "dual eligible" patients (who account for 21% of the Medicare population but 36% of spending).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="10078DBE" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>B. Parkinson Disease &amp; Drug-Induced Parkinsonism</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0CD57636" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="87" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Drug-Induced Parkinsonism (Iatrogenic)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7424B454" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="87" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Do not overlook medications as the culprit. Common offenders include metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, and sodium valproate.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3F26FBFE" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="87" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>Atypical Antipsychotics:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Risperidone and olanzapine frequently cause Parkinsonism.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="559E1451" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="87" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Board Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> If an older patient with dementia (e.g., Lewy Body) develops severe, dangerous agitation requiring an antipsychotic, the agents with the lowest extrapyramidal toxicity are Quetiapine, Pimavanserin (FDA approved for Parkinsonism hallucinations/psychosis, but very expensive), and Clozapine (requires REMS program enrollment).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2C7E477C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="87" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Classic Symptoms</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="038934A5" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="87" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Asymmetric resting tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, postural instability (late finding), hypophonia (soft voice), loss of smell, and micrographia (abnormally small handwriting).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="41B55431" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="87" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Clinical Clue:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Difficulty initiating gait when crossing a threshold (e.g., doorways, elevator doors).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="646BA2C2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="87" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Pharmacologic Management</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="104FC6D8" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="87" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Carbidopa/Levodopa (Sinemet): The most effective medication for Parkinson Disease in the elderly, particularly for gait issues (bradykinesia, rigidity). Tremor response is more variable.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5F8C0231" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="87" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Mechanism &amp; Dosing:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Carbidopa prevents the peripheral breakdown of levodopa. It requires &gt; 75 mg daily for maximum effect.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="193CF4B5" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="87" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Titration:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Begin at 25 mg/100 mg BID or TID. May use 25/250 as the dose is increased. The average patient requires 500 to 1,000 mg of L-dopa/day. Start low and increase every 1-2 weeks. It can take up to 2 months to see full effect (though sometimes immediate). </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Rule:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Optimize the Carbidopa/Levodopa dose completely before adding any other drugs.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5284949A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="87" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Medications to Avoid in the Elderly</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4345B169" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="87" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Anticholinergics and Amantadine have </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>little to no role</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> in treating elderly Parkinson patients.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="63B3DE56" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="87" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>COMT inhibitors and MAO inhibitors are generally very expensive for only modest gains.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0665773E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="87" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Dopamine agonists are highly likely to cause delusions and hallucinations in older adults.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7D9C2B82" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>C. Geriatric Syndromes &amp; Mimics</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6B3E1DF3" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="194" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Restless Leg Syndrome &amp; Koilonychia</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="490FD9B4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="194" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>If a patient presents with sleep difficulty/leg discomfort and the physical exam reveals spoon-shaped nails (koilonychia), suspect Iron Deficiency Anemia causing secondary Restless Leg Syndrome.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="001FB3EC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="194" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Treatment:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Ferrous Sulfate (FeSO4) 325 mg daily. Fixing the iron deficiency effectively resolves the restless legs.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5B461CEE" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="194" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Diarrhea in the Nursing Home</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2BD0B925" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="194" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>The Fecal Impaction Mimic:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> A highly tested scenario. An afebrile patient on opioids and/or anticholinergics who presents with frequent incontinence of "small volumes of liquid stool" does </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> have infectious diarrhea. Distension of the rectum by a fecalith stimulates large bowel contraction, allowing only small amounts of liquid stool to leak past the blockage. The treatment is relieving the constipation.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="015B8AC6" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="194" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Clostridium Difficile Infection</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2FE7889A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="194" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Presents with severe diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever (e.g., 38.5^\circ \text{C}), and leukocytosis.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6070380A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="194" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>First-Line Treatment Options:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Oral Vancomycin 125 mg QID, OR Fidaxomicin. (Fidaxomicin is bactericidal and has lower recurrence rates compared to bacteriostatic Vancomycin, but cost/insurance coverage is often a barrier).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4C22B089" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>D. Sensory Impairments</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="69DB5248" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="36" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Age-Related Macular Degeneration (AMD)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1DF07E2F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="36" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Leading cause of new blindness in patients &gt; 55 years old. Causes severe, irreversible loss of </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>central</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> visual acuity (impairs reading, face recognition, driving) due to the high concentration of photoreceptors in the macula.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2E258560" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="36" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Types:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> "Dry" is more common and can be asymptomatic. "Wet" is more severe and is treated with anti-VEGF injections.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="707A8578" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="36" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Diagnosis:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Monitored using an Amsler Grid. Instruct the patient to report if the straight grid lines begin to appear wavy.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="275F4561" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="36" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Glaucoma</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0CF56C97" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="36" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Characterized by optic neuropathy and visual field changes, causing </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>peripheral</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> visual loss (unlike the central loss of AMD). Usually associated with increased intraocular pressure.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3E5381DF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="36" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Charles Bonnet Syndrome</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="24AA1783" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="36" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Experienced by 10-13% of patients with severe vision loss.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2C54CBB7" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="36" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Presentation:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Purely visual (not auditory) hallucinations. Described as a "phantom limb syndrome for the eyes." Patients will report seeing vivid shapes, unicorns, or people (e.g., "Santas with long beards") but are typically undisturbed by them and lack any other psychiatric complaints.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4C17FE20" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="36" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Management:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Strict reassurance and education. Do </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> order head imaging, urinalysis, or antipsychotics.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4F7CF8E0" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="36" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Presbycusis</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="08648EC2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="36" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>The most common type of hearing loss in older adults.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="14C552BD" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="36" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Characterized by a reduction in hearing thresholds at </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>higher</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> frequencies.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7103E138" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="36" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Counseling Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Do not yell at hearing-impaired older adults, as yelling naturally raises the pitch of your voice. Speak in a lower register. Hearing loss is a major risk factor for developing dementia and must be addressed.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3CE402E7" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>E. Rheumatologic &amp; Cardiovascular Presentations</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="12501E94" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="200" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>Polymyalgia Rheumatica (PMR)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="26B1D242" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="200" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Rare in patients &lt; 50 years old (average age 70).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0EDBF430" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="200" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Diagnosis:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Bilateral aching and morning stiffness involving at least two of the following three areas: 1) Neck/torso, 2) Shoulders/proximal arms, 3) Hips/proximal thighs. Labs will show an ESR &gt; 40 mm/h or an elevated CRP.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2733907C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="200" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Treatment:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> "Low-dose" oral prednisone (e.g., 15 mg daily). Often produces dramatic relief within 24-48 hours. </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Tapering the steroids is a very slow process that can take up to a year (stepping down by 1 mg increments) to avoid relapse.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="003A9D9F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="200" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Giant Cell Arteritis (Temporal Arteritis)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="63BD958E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="200" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Patients with PMR are at high risk of developing GCA.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7B1F5CA6" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="200" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Treatment:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> "High-dose" oral prednisone (e.g., 40-60 mg daily).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7D324263" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="200" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Diagnostic Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Initiate empiric treatment with high-dose steroids immediately upon suspicion to prevent irreversible blindness. A temporal artery biopsy will still remain positive for up to 2 weeks after starting steroids. Ultrasound is increasingly used to replace the disfiguring biopsy.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0C29DA5C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="200" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Postprandial Orthostatic Hypotension</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3CAAC1C8" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="200" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>A syndrome of orthostatic hypotension resulting in syncope or near-syncope occurring 30-45 minutes after a meal. Highly associated with high-carbohydrate meals causing splanchnic blood pooling.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7DD8DEA2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="200" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Prevention:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Counsel the patient to eat smaller meals, remain seated for 45 minutes after eating (perhaps to socialize/gossip before walking to their room), and strictly avoid taking hypotensive medications at mealtime.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="03E1E9E9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><pict ns2:anchorId="6CC5494E"><ns3:rect id="_x0000_i1044" style="width:0;height:1.5pt" ns4:hralign="center" ns4:hrstd="t" ns4:hr="t" fillcolor="#a0a0a0" stroked="f" /></pict></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0CD74945" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00586D48" rsidP="00586D48"><pPr><pStyle val="Style1" /></pPr><r><t>21. GERIATRICS: CAPACITY, ETHICS, &amp; SCREENING</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3A228354" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>A. Decision-Making Capacity &amp; Advance Directives</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="59150E3F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="179" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Legal &amp; Ethical Hierarchy</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="17D073D6" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="179" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Decision-Makers:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> If a patient lacks capacity and has no documented advance directive, states typically follow a hierarchy: Spouse \rightarrow Adult Children \rightarrow Parents \rightarrow Siblings.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="32F89044" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="179" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Administrative Pearls:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> A Power of Attorney </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>cannot</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> be legally appointed after a patient has been admitted and determined to lack capacity. Fiancés and minors (e.g., a 16-year-old grandson) have no legal decision-making authority.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="04C34B09" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="179" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Decision Standards:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> "Substituted Judgment" (making the choice the patient </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>would</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> have made based on prior knowledge) vs. "Best Interest Standard" (acting strictly in the patient's beneficence).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="532252F0" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="179" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Determining Capacity</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="66D4A3A4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="179" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t xml:space="preserve">A diagnosis of dementia does </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> automatically equate to a lack of capacity. If a demented patient can accurately repeat back the risks, benefits, and alternatives of a procedure (e.g., pacemaker placement), they retain capacity for that specific decision.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0696BE59" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="179" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Elements of Capacity:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Understanding, Appreciation, Reasoning, and Choice. Capacity is decision-specific (a patient may have capacity to choose their lunch, but not to consent to major surgery).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="601101CB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="179" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Provider Roles:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Lack of capacity typically requires documentation by two physicians. Use validated tools like the ACE (Aid to Capacity Exam).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="077EC20F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="179" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Legal Distinction:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Physicians determine </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>capacity</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">. Only a judge/court can determine legal </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>competency</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="24E76FD9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>B. Quaternary Prevention (Choosing Wisely) in Geriatrics</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5DBAACB9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="267" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Feeding Tubes in Dementia</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5D812DB1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="267" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">AGS Guideline: Do </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> recommend percutaneous feeding tubes (PEG) in patients with advanced dementia. It does not improve outcomes, increases agitation, and makes the patient miserable. Instead, offer oral-assisted feeding. Loss of appetite is a natural part of the neurologic shutting-down process.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3FC2BC23" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="267" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Anorexia &amp; Cachexia Management</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2CB86694" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="267" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>AGS Guideline: Avoid using prescription appetite stimulants.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="394409C4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="267" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Black Box Warning:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Megestrol (Megace) is on the Beers Criteria. Do not use it; it lacks efficacy and significantly increases the risk of deep vein thrombosis and death.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0237816B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="267" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Intervention:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Optimize social supports, provide appealing foods, and discontinue medications interfering with eating. Encourage consuming protein first at meals to maintain muscle mass. If concurrent depression is present, Mirtazapine is a reasonable off-label consideration.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="266C97DA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="267" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Cancer Screening Discontinuation</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2F0A3F98" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="267" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">AGS Guideline: Do </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> recommend screening for breast, colorectal, prostate, or lung cancer without considering life expectancy.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="373679F1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="267" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>The "10-Year Rule":</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> If a patient's estimated life expectancy is &lt; 10 years, do not pursue routine cancer screening (utilize the validated </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Eprognosis</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> tool from UCSF to calculate).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3FF0F695" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="267" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Specific USPSTF Cutoffs:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Colorectal screening for ages 76-85 is a Grade C. Mammography for age  &gt; 75 is a Grade I (Insufficient). Aspirin for primary CV disease prevention in adults  &gt; 60 is a Grade D.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1A30786A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="267" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Preoperative Assessment</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="50883C85" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="267" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Guideline: Cardiac stress testing or noninvasive imaging is strictly unnecessary before low-risk noncardiac surgery (e.g., cataract surgery) if the patient has no cardiac history and good functional status.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="15869522" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="267" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>Functional Status Metric:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Reaching  &gt; 4 METS (ability to do heavy housework or climb a flight of stairs without stopping).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6713F285" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>C. Elder Abuse &amp; Neglect</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="034BC8DD" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="61" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Screening &amp; Identification</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0D657305" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="61" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">USPSTF: Gives an "I" (Insufficient) rating for routine screening. However, the AMA and Joint Commission </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>do</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> recommend screening for elder mistreatment.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="07FA4B28" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="61" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Risk Factors (Patient): Poor health, lower socioeconomic status, history of abuse, and </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>dementia</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> (cognitively impaired patients are at the greatest risk).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2821FAD3" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="61" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Risk Factors (Perpetrator): Caregiver stress, financial dependence on the elder, history of family violence, and substance abuse.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3AA03A75" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="61" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Clinical Red Flags: Sedation/overmedication, severe dehydration (e.g., Sodium &gt; 147), malnutrition, pressure sores, and unexplained fractures.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2FEF711F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8"
