/></pPr><r><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>23. GERIATRICS: PHARMACOLOGY &amp; PRESCRIBING PRINCIPLES</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1DF1508A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>A. Pharmacokinetics in Aging</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4DAB7534" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="120" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Absorption</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="45BC3923" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="120" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Clinical Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Absorption changes the </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>least</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> with age. It remains relatively stable unless affected by concomitant medications.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="60E8C71C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="120" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Distribution</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5D2B5D61" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="120" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Older adults experience an increased fat-to-water ratio and decreased muscle mass.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="63AA34C4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="120" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Decreased plasma protein (albumin) leads to higher levels of unbound "free" active drug.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="13B73DF0" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="120" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Lipophilia:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Fat-soluble drugs have a wider volume of distribution. For example, switching an 80-year-old from atenolol to propranolol (highly lipophilic) can result in profound bradycardia, confusion, and despondency.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="77CFE7E4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="120" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Metabolism</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7E000F82" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="120" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Decreased liver mass and reduced hepatic blood flow.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="635FEDD4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="120" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Phase I (Oxidation/Cytochrome P450):</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Highly reduced with age. Drugs relying on Phase I (e.g., Diazepam) will accumulate active metabolites, leading to prolonged toxicity (e.g., persistent sedation weeks after discontinuation).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7233C4BD" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="120" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Phase II (Conjugation):</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Minimally affected or unaffected by age. Drugs relying on Phase II (e.g., Lorazepam) are significantly safer choices for older adults.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="470F87FC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="120" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Elimination</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="39E0BC2B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="120" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Clinical Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Elimination changes the </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>most</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> with age (mainly renal).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7D9F8D7E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="120" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Serum Creatinine Fallacy: Serum creatinine is </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>not</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> a reliable estimator of clearance in the elderly. Older, frail adults lack sufficient muscle mass to break down into creatinine, resulting in falsely low serum levels that severely overestimate renal function.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6D040CBD" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="120" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Cockcroft-Gault Equation: You must calculate actual Creatinine Clearance (CrCl), which factors in patient weight and age. (e.g., An 80-year-old, 110-lb woman with a seemingly "normal" creatinine of 1.4 mg/dL actually has a dangerously low CrCl of 23.6 mL/min, compared to a 50-year-old, 150-lb woman with a Cr of 2.2 mg/dL who has a CrCl of 33 mL/min).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4F699323" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>B. Pharmacodynamics &amp; Polypharmacy</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="203E95E2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="66" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Definition of Polypharmacy</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1C08D5B9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="66" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Defined strictly as taking 5 or more medications.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="19FBCCC9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="66" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>AGS Choosing Wisely:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Do not prescribe a new medication without conducting a comprehensive drug review (including OTCs).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6BEC05CC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="66" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Older adults (13-15% of the population) consume 40% of all Over-The-Counter (OTC) purchases. Always ask about "PM" sleep aids when evaluating new-onset confusion, urinary retention, or constipation.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="58A74796" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="66" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>Pharmacodynamics</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="247235A9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="66" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Refers to "what the drug does to the body."</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4C816B03" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="66" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Bleeding Risks:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> An 80-year-old on Warfarin with a therapeutic INR of 2.5 has a significantly higher bleeding risk than a younger patient strictly due to pharmacodynamic vulnerabilities: more friable stomach tissue, higher fall risk, and higher cerebral bleeding risk.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1BF32B08" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>C. The Beers Criteria &amp; High-Risk Medications</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5AC69CAF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Anticholinergic Burden</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1075CA0E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Utilize the Anticholinergic Burden Scale. Decreasing a patient's total score improves cognitive and physical outcomes.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="11D544D2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Paroxetine (Paxil): Extremely highly anticholinergic. Heavily discouraged in older adults.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3DBDD593" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>First-Generation Antihistamines: Strictly avoid diphenhydramine (Benadryl/PM products) for sleep.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="36B74577" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>H2 Blockers: Avoid ranitidine and famotidine if a patient is actively delirious (e.g., in the ICU), as they exacerbate delirium.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2FF0B0A3" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Antibiotics &amp; Renal Cutoffs</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3FB51EDA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Nitrofurantoin (Macrobid): Contraindicated if CrCl &lt; 30 \text{ mL/min} due to peripheral neuropathy and liver toxicity. </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Board Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> While acceptable for acute simple cystitis (if CrCl permits), it is strictly contraindicated for chronic, long-term prophylaxis due to a high risk of fatal pulmonary fibrosis.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="226D6F88" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Cardiovascular &amp; Endocrine Beers Pearls</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5B5C822E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Alpha-Blockers: Avoid terazosin, doxazosin, and prazosin for treating hypertension due to severe orthostasis/fall risk. If treating concurrent BPH, use tamsulosin for the prostate but select a different agent for blood pressure.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="06FB0D62" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Digoxin: Maximum recommended daily dose in the elderly is 0.125 mg.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="683D26F1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Amiodarone: Avoid unless treating Atrial Fibrillation with concurrent CHF or severe LVH.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0D0BCA30" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Sulfonylureas: Explicitly on the Beers list due to high hypoglycemia risk.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="185856BA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Sedatives, Sleep, &amp; Muscle Relaxants</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1003D565" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Benzodiazepines: Avoid for insomnia or agitation. They induce sleep rapidly but block deep REM sleep, preventing memory restoration while massively increasing the risk of falls, MVAs, and hip fractures.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7E1E4387" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">Muscle Relaxants: Avoid carisoprodol and cyclobenzaprine. </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Prescribing Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> If a muscle relaxant is absolutely necessary, select Methocarbamol (Robaxin) because it has the shortest half-life.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6A1188F4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="256" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Antiemetics: Avoid promethazine and metoclopramide. Ondansetron (Zofran) is the safer evidence-based option.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3DBFDCAC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><pict ns2:anchorId="7DE9654D"><ns3:rect id="_x0000_i1048" style="width:0;height:1.5pt" ns4:hralign="center" ns4:hrstd="t" ns4:hr="t" fillcolor="#a0a0a0" stroked="f" /></pict></r></p><p ns2:paraId="048178C5" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00586D48" rsidP="00586D48"><pPr><pStyle val="Style1" /></pPr><r><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>24. GERIATRICS: DISEASE-SPECIFIC MANAGEMENT &amp; SYNDROMES</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7602DF93" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>A. Diabetes Mellitus Goals</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="67480B94" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="155" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Target A1c &amp; Life Expectancy</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="51F10C4C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="155" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>AGS Choosing Wisely:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Avoid using medications other than Metformin to achieve an A1c &lt; 7.5% in most older adults. Moderate control is safer and preferred.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2771B027" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="155" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Time-to-Benefit: The time to see benefit from tight glycemic control (preventing microvascular/renal/ophthalmologic complications) is &gt; 8 years. (Conversely, the time-to-benefit for BP and lipid control is only 2-3 years).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4F7F9B43" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="155" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Target Ranges:</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6D744409" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="155" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Life expectancy &gt; 10 years: Target A1c closer to 7.5%.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="20630049" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="2" /><numId val="155" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Poor life expectancy (e.g., severe COPD, advanced heart failure): Target A1c closer to 8.5% - 9.0%.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="110EC378" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>B. Hypertension Goals &amp; Frailty</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7E6D88FD" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="206" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>JNC-8 vs. SPRINT Trial Nuances</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4B2D4D2B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="206" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>JNC-8:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Recommends a BP goal of &lt; 150/90 for adults  &gt; 60.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="23F63CD2" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="206" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>SPRINT Trial:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Showed better CV outcomes with aggressive targets (Systolic &lt; 120), but treatment caused more AKI, syncope, and hypotension. </t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Critical Limitation:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> SPRINT excluded diabetics, patients with prior strokes, and institutionalized/demented elderly.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="388801BB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="206" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Clinical Translation:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Tighter control (110-120) is appropriate for a robust 80-year-old without side effects. Looser control (&lt; 150/90) is mandatory for frail or severely demented patients in long-term care to prevent catastrophic falls and hypoperfusion.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="54B73AAC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="206" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Frailty Syndrome</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1452CD3A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="206" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Diagnosed by the presence of 3 or more of the following: 1) Weakness, 2) Slowed walking speed, 3) Low physical activity, 4) Low energy/exhaustion, 5) Weight loss.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4797DD65" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="206" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Treatment:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Physical activity and physical therapy are the definitive interventions.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="281555CB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>C. Cognitive Impairment &amp; Dementia</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="55890803" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="88" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Dementia Prevention</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="05054EEF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="88" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Proven protective factors: Higher educational attainment (well-proven), diet, physical activity, and aggressive control of mid-life cardiovascular risk factors (treating diabetes and lipids at age 40).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="37B1D342" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="88" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Sleep Metric:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Patients require at least 5 hours of sleep per night to prevent raising their dementia risk. However, if a patient is using pharmacologic sleep medicine, the risk of dementia remains elevated regardless of the hours slept.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3C0259CD" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="88" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Cholinesterase Inhibitors (Donepezil/Aricept)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="413BEA9C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="88" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>AGS Choosing Wisely:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Do not prescribe without periodic assessment for perceived cognitive benefits and adverse effects.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="32A4340C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="88" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Highly Tested Side Effects:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> GI distress, insomnia, dizziness, and bradycardia. (If a patient on Donepezil presents with a heart rate in the 40s or 50s, the dementia medication is a likely culprit, not just standard AV-nodal blocking cardiac drugs).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="606D6DE0" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="88" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Serotonin Syndrome in the Elderly</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="611D0915" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="88" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Watch for dangerous drug interactions. Adding Tramadol (often prescribed for compression fractures) to a patient already on SSRIs (Sertraline) and sleep aids (Trazodone) frequently precipitates Serotonin Syndrome.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="31F25B3F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="88" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Presentation:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Hyperreflexia, mild tremor, loose stools, and low-grade fever.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="39A359F4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>D. Dental Prophylaxis Guidelines</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4ABEA5DB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="280" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Prosthetic Joints (ADA Guidelines)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0EED8F97" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="280" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Patients with prosthetic joints (e.g., hip or knee replacements) do NOT require prophylactic antibiotics prior to dental extractions/procedures unless there are specific, severe immunocompromising conditions present.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4AAA3CD7" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="280" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Prophylaxis (e.g., Amoxicillin 2g) remains appropriate for prosthetic cardiac valves, a history of infective endocarditis, and specific congenital heart abnormalities.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7FECEBA7" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="008370D8" rsidRPr="00D26483" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><pict ns2:anchorId="2449687A"><ns3:rect id="_x0000_i1049" style="width:0;height:1.5pt" ns4:hralign="center" ns4:hrstd="t" ns4:hr="t" fillcolor="#a0a0a0" stroked="f" /></pict></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3F1966EB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="0066644A" rsidRDefault="00000000"><pPr><pStyle val="Heading1" /></pPr><r><t>Session 21: Hematology Issues</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2BFD19EF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><pBdr><top val="single" sz="12" color="1F3864" /></pBdr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="1F3864" /><spacing refs</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0ABDF3C7" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="2E75B6" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="16" /><szCs val="16" /><color val="FFFFFF" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="16" /><szCs val="16" /><color val="FFFFFF" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  ITE EXAM QUESTIONS</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="74EC0F6B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="D6E4F7" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="1F3864" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="1F3864" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Q1   2025   •   Q2025-083   (match score: 4.99, 11 keyword hits)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="BC8FDE85" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="D6E4F7" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="444444" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="444444" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Initial anticoagulation for unprovoked DVT Deep Vein Thrombosis Management</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2392FB96" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="D6E4F7" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="1F3864" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs val="444444" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  DVT anticoagulation in advanced CKD Deep Vein Thrombosis Management</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="9B23C786" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="2E75B6" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="16" /><szCs val="16" /><color /><color val="FFFFFF" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  AAFP Poll Questions — Session 21: Hematology Issues</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="0" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="7F4F24" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  6 practice questions from the AAFP Board Prep presentation. Work each question before checking the answer key.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  1.  A 38-year-old female patient presents with pain in her R calf of 3 days duration. She had a lap cholecystectomy 8 weeks ago and a cervical conization for carcinoma-in-situ 6 years ago with no recurrence. No one in her family ever had a DVT. She has 2+ bilateral pitting ankle edema. Your choice of an initial diagnostic study would be: Diagnosing DVT • D-dimer (ELISA) – Cut off &lt;500 mcg/L • Consider age x 10 for &gt;50 years old • Increase in false positive with age – 95% sensitive, 40-60% specific – 95% negative predictive value – Excellent test to rule out, not so good to rule in! Schouten HJ, et al. Diagnostic accuracy of conventional or age adjusted D-dimer cut-off values in older patients with suspected venous thromboembolism: systematic review and meta-analysis. BMJ2013;346:f2492 SORT B J Am CollCardiol. 2017;70(19):2411 Diagnosing DVT • Normal D-dimer and low probability Well’s Score= DVT unlikely • Moderate/ High prob Wells score: get U/S (D-dimer NOT helpful) • Venous compression ultrasounds have 94% PPV • If negative and high clinical suspicion, repeat US at 5-7 days • Best sensitivity and specificity in symptomatic pts with proximal thrombosis of lower extremities • Limitations: new vs old clot, isolated pelvic clot can be missed tumor/ abscess false + Am Fam Physician.2012Nov15;86(10):913-919 SORT C If VTE Is Diagnosed, Who Needs… • Screening for malignancy?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  D-dimer</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Proteins C and S</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Venous compression ultrasounds</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Venogram</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 21</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  2.  A 63-year-old man presents with a history of several days of shortness of breath with exertion and pleuritic chest pain. He suffered a DVT 5 years ago. His pO = 85% on RA and his HR is 2 110. He recently returned from vacation in Japan. What is the best test for initial evaluation of this patient’s symptoms?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  D-dimer</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Compression US of the lower extremities</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  VQ scanning</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  CT angiography</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 21</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  3.  A 48-year-old female presents with 3 days of left lower extremity swelling. Venous Dopplers reveal a proximal DVT. She is stable and has no other medical problems. What would be the best initial treatment for this patient?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Rivaroxaban (Xarelto) only as an outpatient</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Low-molecular-weight heparin for 5 days and Rivaroxaban</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Hospitalization for unfractionated heparin and warfarin</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Hospitalization for thrombolytic therapy</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 21</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  4.  A 28-year-old pregnant female, G3P2, is diagnosed with a DVT in her right lower extremity. The treatment of choice for this patient would be: VTE in Pregnancy  VTE risk increases 5-fold in pregnancy  Risk 1/1,600 pregnancies  PE is a leading cause of preg-associated mortality/ morbidity  20-50% have an underlying thrombophilia  History of VTE: 3-4 x increased risk for recurrence during preg VTE in Pregnancy • Risk of VTE overall, antepartum = postpartum and occurs with equal frequency throughout all trimesters • PE is more common in the postpartum period, especially 1st wk • D-dimer less useful in pregnancy (almost always positive) • Lower dose chest CTPA safe in pregnancy – If not available, do CXR – If CXR is normal, do V/Q scan – If CXR is abnormal, do full dose CT scan Anticoagulation in Pregnancy • Heparin and LMWH do not cross placenta and are safe in pregnancy SORT B • Unfractionated Heparin (UH) – Dosage requirements increase due to increases in heparin binding proteins, plasma volume, renal clearance and degradation by the placenta • Low-Molecular-Weight Heparin (LMWH) – Less bleeding, osteoporosis and HIT in non-pregnant pts compared to UH – Longer half-life – longer dosage intervals, but increased risk if epidural/spinal – Epidural anesthesia 12 hours after last dose of LMWH SORT C ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 196. ObstetGynecol2018;132(1):p e1–e17. Anticoagulation in Pregnancy • Warfarin- not for use in pregnancy – Crosses the placenta – Associated with harmful fetal effects – Use postpartum or in some patients with mechanical heart valves • DOACS not adequately studied in pregnancy • All anticoagulants are safe for breastfeeding mothers • LMWH and coumadin with good data • DOACs- limited data ACOG Bulletin #196-ObstetGynecol2018;132(1):p e1–e17. SORT B Anticoagulation in Pregnancy • Convert from LMWH to UH for last month of pregnancy • Compression stockings until anticoagulation resumed postpartum • Restart anticoagulation after delivery – 4-6 hours post vaginal delivery – 6-12 hours post C-section • May be bridged to warfarin or DOACs postpartum • Avoid paradoxical thrombosis and skin necrosis from early anti- protein C effect of warfarin by bridging with LMWH or UFH for 5 days and INR 2-3 x 2 days ACOG Bulletin #196-ObstetGynecol2018;132(1):p e1–e17. SORT B. VTE and COVID in pregnancy • If hospitalized, VTE prophylaxis while hospitalized continue for 2 weeks after discharge • No data on those managed as outpatients • National Institutes of Health COVID-19 Treatment Guidelines • ACOG: COVID-19 FAQs for Obstetrician-Gynecologists, Obstetrics Contraception if history of VTE • Avoid Estrogen • Use of progesterone is controversial • NIH: ok with first DVT, but not if recurrent • Depo Provera may have the highest risk but not well studied</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Apixaban (Eliquis)</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Unfractionated heparin and warfarin</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Low-molecular-weight heparin</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Vena cava filter</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 21</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  5.  A 48-year-old female with rheumatoid arthritis presents to your office complaining of two months of fatigue, and more recently dyspnea on exertion. Her Hgb=9.2 with normocytic, normochromic indices. You order iron studies, which show low iron, low TIBC and high ferritin. The best treatment for this patient would be: 12 Kinetic Approach to Anemia • Two reasons for anemia: – Decreased RBC production (reticulocyte &lt; 2) • Lack of substrate • Marrow disorders/suppression • Decreased trophic hormones • Chronic illness/inflammation – Increased RBC loss • Hemolysis – inherited or acquired (reticulocyte &gt; 2) • Blood loss – occult or obvious Morphologic Approach to Anemia • RBC indices – reflect cell size – Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) – Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) – Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC) – Red cell Distribution Width (RDW) • Use the RBC indices to direct your work-up of anemia Morphologic Approach to Anemia • Macrocytic (MCV &gt;100) – Reticulocytosis (high RDW) – B and folate deficiencies 12 • PPIs and metformin can cause B12 deficiency – Myelodysplasia Courtesy: Wikimedia Commons – Alcohol abuse, liver disease, hypothyroidism Morphologic Approach to Anemia • Microcytic Anemia (MCV &lt;80) – Iron deficiency – Decreased heme synthesis • lead, sideroblastic anemia – Decreased globin synthesis By Dr Graham Beards (Own work) [CC BY-SA 3.0 (thalassemia, hemoglobinopathy) (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0)], via Wikimedia Commons – Chronic illness/inflammation (uncommon) Morphologic Approach to Anemia • Normocytic, normochromic anemia – Acute blood loss – Acute hemolysis (retic &gt;2) – Hypersplenism – Anemia of chronic disease (most common) Iron Deficiency Anemia • Low iron, high iron binding capacity, low ferritin • Identify WHY the patient is iron deficient! – Menstrual blood loss – GI malignancy/blood loss • Response to iron therapy: • Retic count 5-7 days • Hct 4-6 weeks • Iron stores 4-6 months *Low dose iron (15mg elemental iron)- effective, less side effects- SOR C Am Fam Physician.2018;98(7):437-442</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Oral B for fatigue</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Oral ferrous sulfate for anemia</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Home oxygen for dyspnea</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  DMARDs for rheumatoid arthritis</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 21</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  6.  A 3-year-old patient is brought to see you after moving to your community. He has Hgb SS disease. The parents ask you whether he needs to continue taking the prophylactic penicillin prescribed by another physician. You should recommend: Sickle Cell Disease • Most common single gene disorder in African-Americans • Diagnosed by hemoglobin electrophoresis • Due to homozygous abnormal hemoglobin S (SS disease) – Sickle SC disease (less severe disease, more retinal disease) – Sickle-Thal (variable) • Hemoglobin S is poorly soluble when deoxygenated and forms polymers that deforms the cells Sickle Cell Disease • Clinical Manifestations – Mod-severe anemia (hgb &lt;8) w/ high retic count, normal/ high MCV – Vaso-occlusive crises – muscular, CVA, renal, priapism, retinopathy • Tx: IV narcotics if severe pain (SOR-B), NSAIDs if mild-moderate pain – Infections – pneumococcus, hemophilus, salmonella – Aplastic crisis – parvovirus B19 marrow suppression – Acute chest syndrome – pneumonia + infarct – Splenic sequestration crisis – acute anemia Sickle Cell Disease • Infection Prophylaxis – Immunizations • Strep pneumonia (PCV13 and 20) • Neisseria meningitides (Menactra and Men-B) • H. influenza, Type B • Hepatitis B • Influenza – Penicillin prophylaxis (for all types of SSD) • Pen V p.o. 125 mg BID from age 3 month to 3 years • Pen V p.o. 250 mg BID from age 3 to 5 years • After age 5 is controversial Sickle Cell Disease Annual Screening with Transcranial Doppler Ultrasound to evaluate risk of stroke age 2-16 years (SORT A) Bone Marrow/ Stem Cell Transplant-potentially curative Hydroxyurea: age 9-42 months or adults who have 3 or more crises in 12 months decrease vaso-occlusive effects and risk of acute chest syndrome (SORT B) Disease modifying therapy (less VOC, ACS, mortality, transfusion, hosp) Increases levels of HbF Initiation requires contraceptive counseling, CBC and reticulocyte q4w Risks: neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, gets into breast milk, teratogen Am Fam Phys 2015 Dec 15; 92(12): 1069-1076 Multiple Myeloma • Incidence: 1.6% of all cancer, 10% of hematologic cancer in U.S. • Risk factors: age &gt;65, African Americans • Signs/ sx: anemia (73%) bone pain (58%) elevated creatinine (48%) hypercalcemia (28%) N/V, weakness, recurrent infections, weight loss Multiple Myeloma Dx: abnormal plasma cells in marrow (immature, atypical) monoclonal protein in serum/ urine bone lesions Work Up: • Labs: CBC w/ diff, Cr, LDH, beta-2 macroglobulin, immunoglobulin studies • Skeletal survey • BM biopsy AM Fam Physician. 2017 Mar 15:95(6): 373-383A. Multiple Myeloma Treatment: • Primary care: Bisphosphonate txwhen first diagnosed DVT prevention-DOAC or LMWH Infection prevention • Prophylactic ABX (TMP/ SMZ + quinolones 1st3 moof tx) • PCN for recurrent pneumococcal infections • Vaccines: PCV, Hib, flu IVIG for serious infections Avoid nephrotoxic meds Nutrition support Pain control • Referral to oncology: chemo, stem cell transplant AM Fam Physician. 2017 Mar 15:95(6): 373-383A. Hemophilia • Inherited bleeding disorders – Hemophilia A – Factor VIII deficiency – Hemophilia B – Factor IX deficiency (Christmas Disease) • X-linked recessive – predominantly males affected • Become symptomatic within first two years of life – Only 50% have bleeding with circumcision – 3-5% have bleeding in perinatal period Hemophilia • Bleeding sites – Muscles – Hematuria – Gastrointestinal – Epistaxis and oral cavity – Joints • Late joint destruction • Joints preserved by early initiation of factor concentrate treatments Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura “ Pentad” • Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia • Thrombocytopenia usually with purpura • Acute renal insufficiency (HUS) • Neurological symptoms (TTP) • Fever • Actually rare for all five to be present Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura • Causes – Usually idiopathic – ADAMTS13 deficiency • Protease of VWF – inhibitory auto-antibody • Long polymers of VWF attract platelets – Shiga-toxin from E. coli 0157:H7 – Collagen vascular diseases, cancer and transplants – Medications • Ticlopidine, clopidogrel, quinine, mitomycin, tacrolimus • Treatment – plasma exchange therapy curative Leukemia • Acute Leukemia – Acute Lymphoblastic – Children • Fever, bleeding, musculoskeletal symptoms, • Hepatosplenomegaly (75%), lymphadenopathy (60%) – Acute Myelogenous –Adults • Fever, fatigue, weight-loss, bleeding problems • Anemia, thrombocytopenia • Rouleaux formation on smear Leukemia • Chronic Leukemias – Adults • Most asymptomatic at time of diagnosis • Discovered with leukocytosis on blood count • Work Up: repeat CBC, smear, flow cytometry, onc referral • Chronic lymphocytic leukemia – 50% • Hepatosplenomegaly and lymphadenopathy • Chronic myelogenous leukemia – 20% • Splenomegaly Am Fam Physician.2014;89(9):731-738 Board Frequent Fliers • D Dimer only helpful for low pretest probability • if not CTPA for PE and doppler for DVT • DOAC preferred over coumadin (LMWH if cancer) • Dose adjust DOACs with renal disease • No coumadin in pregnancy (DOACs with insufficient data) • Anemia of Chronic DZ: treat underlying DZ • Macrocytic anemia- from PPIs due to B12 deficiency • Sickle Cell: transcranial U/S screening • Multiple Myeloma: start bisphosphonate tx • TTP pentad: fever, anemia, thrombocytopenia, AKI, neuro sx • Acute Leukemia: fever, bleeding • Chronic Leukemia: asymptomatic, elevated WBC-work up: repeat WBC, smear, flow cytometry, oncreferral Answers 1. A 2. D 3. A 4. C 5. D 6. C</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Stop the penicillin to avoid antibiotic resistance</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Take penicillin V 125 mg daily for the rest of his life</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Take penicillin V 250 mg BID until age 5 at least</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Take penicillin V 250 mg daily until age 12</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 21</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="40" after="120" /><shd val="clear" fill="7F4F24" color="auto" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color
