/><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="444444" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  First-line preventive treatment for cluster headaches Cluster Headache Prophylaxis</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3E280431" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="D6E4F7" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="1F3864" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="1F3864" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Q5   2024   •   Q2024-862   (match score: 1.30, 3 keyword hits)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1025EEEA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="D6E4F7" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="444444" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="444444" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Recognizing febrile seizure in young child Febrile Seizure</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="F126336A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="2E75B6" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="16" /><szCs val="16" /><color val="FFFFFF" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="16" /><szCs val="16" /><color val="FFFFFF" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  KEY REFERENCES</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="120" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="7F4F24" color="auto" /></pPr><r><rPr><b /><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="20" /><szCs val="20" /><color val="FFFFFF" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  AAFP Poll Questions — Session 26: Common Neurological Disorders</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="0" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="7F4F24" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  7 practice questions from the AAFP Board Prep presentation. Work each question before checking the answer key.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  1.  A 65-year-old male patient of yours presents to the ED having had a seizure in his bedroom witnessed by his wife. She heard a cry and the fall, saw him stiffen and shake all over, and then become incontinent of urine. He was not arousable until he had been in the ED for several minutes. His seizure would be classified as: Definition A seizure is a transient occurrence of signs or symptoms due to abnormal excessive or synchronous neuronal activity in the brain, and can be either focal or generalized 10% of adults have a SZ during their lifetime • 1/3 will have a recurrence within 1 yr of an initial unprovoked SZ Classification of Seizures • Focal seizures (formerly partial) – Local – Ipsilateral propagation – Contralateral propagation – Secondarily generalized – Consciousness may or may not be impaired (formerly complex vs simple) Classification of Seizures • Generalized seizures – Non-convulsive (absence) • Typical (3/sec spike and slow waves on EEG) • Atypical (&lt;3/sec spike and slow waves on EEG) – Convulsive • Myoclonic (very short muscle contractions) • Clonic (repeated jerking) • Tonic (stiffening) • Tonic-clonic (stiffening followed by jerking) – Grand Mal term is now outdated and no longer used • Atonic (“drop attacks” – loss of muscle tone) • PNES- Psychogenic Non-Epileptic Seizures (former pseudo-seizures)</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Complex Partial</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Generalized Tonic-Clonic</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Grand Mal</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Myoclonic</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 26</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  2.  The most likely cause of a new seizure in a patient &gt;65 years old would be: Etiology of Seizures • Idiopathic – 62% overall ages • Stroke – 15% overall, 49% &gt;age 60 • Brain tumor – 6% overall, 11% &gt;age 60 • Head trauma • Intracranial infection • COVID • 55% more SZs in first 6 mo vs those who had influenza- stronger association in kids • Cerebral degeneration • Congenital brain malformations Family Medicine Today November 2022 • Inborn errors of metabolism “Provoked” Seizures • Seizures that occur within a medical setting, that, if removed, presumably the seizures would not occur • Metabolic derangements – Hypo and hyperglycemia – Hyponatremia – Hypocalcemia (usually neonates) – Renal failure and uremia “Provoked” Seizures • More rare metabolic causes – Hyperthyroidism – Acute intermittent porphyria • Cerebral anoxia – Arrest, anesthesia, drowning, CO – Syncope with brief hypoventilation (adolescents) • Drug toxicities/withdrawal – Alcohol – Benzodiazepines Epilepsy Epilepsy is a chronic condition characterized by at least two unprovoked seizures at least 24 hours apart.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Cerebrovascular disease/stroke</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Idiopathic</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Metabolic derangement</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Brain tumor</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 26</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  3.  A patient with a new onset seizure has a complete workup that is unremarkable for any provoked causes, signs of infection, drug toxicities, or neurological disease. The next step in the workup for this patient would be: Diagnostic Evaluation • Must start with a complete H&amp;P – Witnessed description of the event – Substance use – Head trauma – Cerebrovascular events – Cardiac history – Sleep disorders • Insomnia lowers SZ threshold due to sleep deprivation • Hypnogogic twitching is not a SZ Medication History Am Fam Physician.2022;105(5):507-513 Diagnostic Evaluation • Blood studies – CBC – Electrolytes • Calcium, magnesium, phosphorus – Glucose – BUN/creatinine – ?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Event monitor</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Prolactin level</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Neuroimaging (CT/MRI)</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Lumbar puncture</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 26</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  4.  A patient presents to you having had a single new unprovoked seizure with negative workup. Your first step in treatment would be: Starting Antiepileptic Drugs (AEDs) • Not needed if single new onset seizure or infrequent seizures • Immediate AED therapy compared to delay of tx pending a 2nd seizure is likely to reduce recurrence risk within the first 2 years (SORT B) but not improve quality of life (SORT C) • Immediate AED unlikely to improve prognosis over a longer term (&gt; 3 years) (SORT B) • Treat only if there is a high risk of recurrence Neurology. 2015; 84(16): 1705 JAMA Neurol. 2018 75(3): 279-286 High Risk of Seizure Recurrence • Status epilepticus • Hx of brain injury • Brain lesion on neuroimaging • Focal neurological abnormalities • Intellectual disability • Abnormal EEG with epileptiform discharges • High-risk seizure types • Focal, absence, myoclonic, atonic; nocturnal SZ Am Fam Physician.2022;105(5):507-513 Antiepileptic Drugs • Start with AED monotherapy • Selection is individualized based on: – Relative efficacy – Other medications (enzyme induction) – Comorbid conditions – Potential adverse effects, tolerability, serious toxicity – Patient preferences and cost • No combination therapy until patient has failed two monotherapy trials • 1/3 of patients have uncontrolled epilepsy despite multiple agents • Monitor patients closely for effectiveness, side effects, and adherence Epilepsia. 2006 July; 47 (7): 1094-120 SORT A NeurolClin. 2010 Nov; 28 (4): 843-52 Classification of AEDs Broad Spectrum All seizure types Narrow Spectrum Focal or secondarily generalized seizures First Generation Less expensive More side effects and drug interactions Require monitoring of drug levels Second Generation More expensive Fewer side effects and interactions Monitoring often unavailable Antiepileptic Drugs First Generation Second Generation More complicated pharmacokinetics Fewer serious adverse effects Narrower therapeutic ranges Wider therapeutic Ranges Less expensive Lower Risk teratogenesis More expensive Examples: Exampes: Carbamazepine Levetiracitam Ethosuxamide Lamotrigine Phenobarbital Oxcarbazepine Phenytoin Gabapentin Primidone Pregabalin ValproicAcid Topiramate Antiepileptic Drugs: First-Line Monotherapy Seizure type &lt; 16 years old 16-59 years old &gt; 60 years Comments old Focal Oxcarbazepine Carbamazepine Gabapentin SORT A Levetiracetam Lamotrigine Phenytoin Zonisamide Generalized: Ethosuxamide No SORT A No SORT A Choose a Broad Absence ValproicAcid spectrum 2nd generation Generalized: No SORTA No SORTA No SORTA Recommendations Others are SORT C/D Am Fam Phys2017 July 15; 96 (2): 87-96 Toxicities of AEDs • Common toxicities with AEDs- 31% report adverse effects – Suicidality –2x risk compared to placebos – Neuro-toxicities – ataxia, dizziness, somnolence, fatigue, HA – Rash – wide spectrum from simple maculopapular rashes to Stevens-Johnson Syndrome – Liver enzyme – induction or inhibition Am Fam Physician.2022;105(5):507-513</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Start no medications at this time</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Start oral valproic acid (Depakene)</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Start oral phenytoin (Dilantin)</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Start oral levetiracetam (Keppra)</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 26</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  5.  A 46-year-old male patient of your practice presents with fever, arthralgias, and erythroderma. He has no respiratory symptoms. His medications include lisinopril, valproic acid, and atenolol. His fever is 102.2o and his skin reveals a very erythematous maculopapular rash and some tenderness. There are no oral lesions. Bloodwork reveals a WBC of 16,000 with 17% eosinophils, and AST and ALT about 2x normal.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 26</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  6.  The most likely diagnosis is: DRESS Syndrome • Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms • Maculopapular rash, fever, arthralgia, lymphadenopathy • Persists weeks to months with 10-25% mortality • Treatment: supportive, stop offending agent • Common offenders: – Carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, valproate – Allopurinol and sulfonamides Am J Med. 2011 Jul; 124(7): 588-97 Status Epilepticus A single unremitting seizure lasting &gt;5 minutes or recurrent seizures without interictal return to baseline • Convulsive: rhythmic jerking of extremities (Generalized Convulsive SE) • Non-convulsive: seizure activity on EEG w/o GCSE • Refractory: do not respond to standard tx for SE NeurocriticalCare. Aug 2012; 17 (1): 3-33. Non-Convulsive Status Epilepticus “Wandering confused” Acutely ill w/ severely impaired mental status +/- subtle motor movements (rhythmic eye twitches, tonic eye deviation) “subtle status” Frequently follows uncontrolled GCSE Often in ICU setting NeurocriticalCare. Aug 2012; 17 (1): 3-33. Mortality Rates Status Epilepticus Type Mortality Rate at Hospital Discharge Convulsive 9-21% Non-Convulsive 18-52% Refractory 23-61% NeurocriticalCare. Aug 2012; 17 (1): 3-33. Status Epilepticus Initial Therapy • Evaluate causes while you treat • Drug of choice: lorazepam 4mg IV x1 • Drug of choice via other routes: • Midazolam IM • Diazepam rectally • Urgent Control Therapy with AED in all unless immediate cause is known and corrected (eg., hypoglycemia) with phenytoin/fosphenytoin, phenobarbital, valproate sodium NeurocriticalCare. Aug 2012; 17 (1): 3-33. SORT A Seizures and Driving • 0.01-0.1% of MVAs are attributable to seizures • Fatal MVAs 8x greater from alcohol than seizures • Seizure-free interval is best indicator of driving safety – The longer the seizure-free interval, the less likely an MVA – The longer the patient is restricted from driving, the lower the adherence • 1994 consensus statement of the American Academy of Neurology, American Epilepsy Society, and the Epilepsy Foundation of America recommend: 3-month SZ-free interval before driving resumed Consensus statements, sample statutory provisions, and model regulations regarding driver licensing and epilepsy. American Academy of Neurology, American Epilepsy Society, and Epilepsy Foundation of America. Epilepsia1994; 35:696 Seizures and Driving • States authorities make the ultimate decision about driving, not physicians • State laws vary regarding seizure-free intervals • Only a few states require physicians to report</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Toxic shock syndrome</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  DRESS syndrome</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Stevens-Johnson syndrome</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Neurocutaneous phakomatosis syndrome</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 26</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  7.  A 46-year-old male smoker comes to you for a complaint of unilateral, cyclical, nocturnal headaches that last 30 minutes each. He has taken OTC meds but they have not been helpful. The best prophylactic medication for his headaches would be: Primary Headaches • Tension-type • Trigeminal Autonomic Cephalgia – Primary, unilateral headache in trigeminal nerve distribution – Ipsilateral autonomic symptoms – Cluster headaches are the most common • Migraines • Others: cough, exercise, sexual activity, etc Chronic Tension Headaches Bilateral, band like Tx: OTC analgesics • if &gt;2x/week, increased risk of chronic daily HA • TCAs for prophylaxis (SOR B) • Butalbital and opioids should be avoided- increases risk of chronic daily HA Chronic Daily Headaches: 15 or more HA per month x 3 months • Can be due to chronic tension headaches or chronic migraines • Treatment: stop all abortive treatments, begin prophylactic treatments: • Non-pharmacologic: biofeedback/ relaxation, cognitive behavioral therapy (SOR B) • Pharmacologic: SOR A: topiramate, valproate SOR B: amitriptyline, gabapentin, tizanidine no effect: SSRIs, propranolol, botox Am Fam Physician 2014:89(8): 642-648. Cluster Headaches • Prevalence &lt;1%, male&gt; female 2-3:1 • Orbital or temporal unilateral pain Last 15 minutes to 3 hours • Ptosis, miosis, rhinorrhea, lacrimation or conjunctival injection • Triggers: alcohol, NTG, nitrates in foods, strong odors • Acute tx– oxygen and SQ/ intranasal triptans- SOR A • 2nd line: somatostatin receptor agonists, nasal lidocaine • Transitional tx: suboccipital steroid injxn- SOR A • Preventive treatment – Verapamil – tx of choice (240-320 mg per day or more)- SOR A – 2nd line: steroids, lithium, topiramate, melatonin – refractory: galcanezumab, neurostimulation- vagal/ deep brain Am Fam Physician.2022;105(1):24-32 Migraine Headache • Affects 18-26% women, 6-9% men in US • Pathophysiology – unclear – Cellular and blood-brain permeability changes inflammation of meninges and vasodilation – Genetic factors play a major role • Recurrent attacks in four phases – Prodrome: euphoria, depression, irritability, yawning – Aura: Visual – flickering lights, spots or lines Sensory – pins and needles, numbness Dysphasic speech – Headache – Postdrome – sudden head movement causes head pain ICHD-3 Criteria: Migraine without Aura 1. unilateral location 2. pulsating/ throbbing quality 3. moderate or severe pain intensity 4. *aggravation by or causing avoidance of routine physical activity (e.g. walking or climbing stairs) 1. nausea and/or vomiting 2. *photophobia and phonophobia E. Not better accounted for by another ICHD-3 diagnosis International Classification of Headache Disorders ICHD-3 Criteria: Migraine WITH Aura 1. at least one aura symptom spreads gradually over ≥5 minutes 2. two or more aura symptoms occur in succession 3. each individual aura symptom lasts 5-60 minutes 4. at least one aura symptom is unilateral 5. at least one aura symptom is positive 6. the aura is accompanied, or followed within 60 minutes, by headache Diagnosis of Migraine Headache • Clinical diagnosis – primarily history • Negative neurological examination • Neuroimaging unnecessary, except with – Unexplained abnormal neuro findings – Headaches that do not fit the strict definition – Choose Wisely Campaign (Radiology) • No benefit for EEG – Choose Wisely Campaign (Neurology) “Red Flags” for Neuroimaging • Rapidly increasing headache frequency • Lack of coordination, abnl neuro exam, papilledema • Headaches that awaken from sleep • Worse headache of life • Patients with cancer, HIV, Lyme disease • New onset of migraines over age 50 • Rapid onset with strenuous exercise • CT or MRI w/o contrast- test of choice (contrast if immunocompromised/ cancer, temporal arteritis, dissection, aneurysm) Am Fam Physician2013 May 15;87(10): 682-687 Acute Treatment of Migraine: First Line • Acetaminophen, NSAIDS • Triptans – serotonin 1b/1d agonists – All triptans have been found to be effective and well-tolerated – Avoid triptans (due to increased BP risk) in: • Familial hemiplegic migraine • Basilar migraine • Ischemic stroke • CAD, Prinzmetal’s angina, uncontrolled HTN • Pregnancy (relative contraindication) • Acetaminophen/aspirin/caffeine • Sumatriptan/naproxen Am Fam Physician. 2018 Feb 15; 97 (4): 243-251 Acute Treatment of Migraine: Second Line • Anti-emetics: dopamine antagonists • Metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, promethazine • Risk extrapyramidal adverse effects • Intranasal Dihydroergotamine (DHE) • Side effects: leg cramps, HTN, tinging in extremities • Contraindicated in pregnancy • Opioids: tramadol, codeine, meperidine, butorphanol • Moderate effectiveness but high abuse potential • Routine use not recommended Am Fam Physician. 2018 Feb 15; 97 (4): 243-251 Refractory Acute Migraine Parenteral Dihydroergotamine (DHE45) • Avoid for 24 hours after triptan • Severe N/V, HTN Magnesium sulfate IV: alternate tx for migraine with aura Dexamethasone IV: consider to decrease recurrence- prednisone taper x10-14 d Valproate IV: contradictory evidence Diphenhydramine: not recommended Opioids: use sparingly and infrequently Am Fam Physician. 2018 Feb 15; 97 (4): 243-251 Preventive Treatment of Migraine • Indications- less than 1 in 5 who qualify are receiving prevention – Headaches significantly interfere with usual activities – Overuse of acute therapies – Adverse events with acute therapies – Patient preference – Certain migraine variants that mimic CVAs • Hemiplegic migraines • Basilar migraines • Prolonged aura Preventive Treatment of Migraine • First line agents (if no contraindications): SORT A • valproic acid • frovatriptan (menstrual migraines) • propranolol, timolol, metoprolol • topiramate • If not effective after 2-3 months, adjust dose • If max dose or adverse effects, change med *If no single first line agent effective/tolerable, consider combination • Second line agents: amitriptyline, atenolol, nadolol, naratriptan, zolmitriptan, venlafaxine, supplements (butterbur, riboflavin, feverfew, magnesium) Am Fam Physician 2019 Jan 1;99(1)17-24 Refractory Chronic Migraines CGRP Receptor Antagonists • Calcitonin Gene Related Peptide- small protein highly prevalent in sensory nerves of head and neck • Examples: atogepant (Quilipta), erenumab (Aimovig), eptinezumab (Vyepti), fremanezumab (Ajovy), glacanezumab (Emgality) • Indications: Abortive- triptan intolerance, failure 2 triptans Preventative- failed 2 agents and have moderate-severe disability Remote Electrical Neuromodulation Botox American Headache Society Consensus Statement June 23, 2021 Estrogen-Containing Contraceptives and Migraines Possible increased risk of CVA if: • migraines with aura at any age • migraines over age 35 (Lack of good quality data but was still on recent ITE) Multiple Sclerosis • Auto-immune inflammatory demyelinating disease • Affects adults 20-40 years old, 2:1 female to male ratio • Most common neurologic disability in young adults • Prevalence 1 per 800 persons; incidence 2-10 per 100,000 • Discrete lesions in various parts of the CNS 3+ months apart • Pathogenesis unclear – Immune system stimuli – Viral infections-mono – Geographic and environmental factors-higher risk further from equator – Genetic factors Am Fam Physician.2022;106(2):173-183 Multiple Sclerosis • Clinical Manifestations – Insidious or sudden onset – Optic neuritis – pain with eye movement and visual loss – Paresthesias, weakness, loss of coordination – Bladder urgency/retention – Constipation, sexual dysfunction – Fatigue and depression – Lhermitte’s sign – neck flexion causes electric shocks down limbs and spine – Uhthoff’s phenomenon – worsening of sx with increased body temperature – No single specific diagnostic test Multiple Sclerosis • Patterns: – Relapsing Remitting– most common and best prognosis – Primary Progressive – Secondary Progressive- smoking increases the risk (SOR B) – Clinically Isolated Syndrome – Radiologically Isolated Syndrome • Acute exacerbations treated with corticosteroids – Methylprednisolone 1000 mg daily x 3 days – Oral and IV steroids equal efficacy (SOR A) – If refractory, consider plasmapheresis Am Fam Physician.2022;106(2):173-183 Multiple Sclerosis • No medication treatment has been shown to affect long term outcome • Tobacco cessationdecrease in disability progression (SOR B) • Disease-modifying agents should be started EARLY • Decreased relapse rate, decreased brain lesions on MRI • Immunomodulators- side effects: Interferons – flu-like syndrome, leukopenia, depression/suicidality Glatiramer – injection site reactions, flushing, chest tightness/palpitations • Immunosuppressives-side effects: Dimethyl fumarate – progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy Fingolimod – zoster infections Teriflunomide –hepatoxicity Am Fam Physician.2022;106(2):173-183 Mitoxantrone – cardiac toxicity Board Frequent Fliers • SZ cause- most common idiopathic overall, if &gt;60 CVA • AED- lots of side effects, not needed after first SZ with neg eval • Epilsepsy in preg- stop 6 mo prior to conception if no SZ &gt;2yrs • Chronic tension HA- TCAs for prevention • Chronic daily HA- stop abortive, start topiramate or valproate, consider biofeedback/CBT • Migraine prevention- TCAs no longer first line • HA neuroimaging- for red flags only- usually CT or MRI w/o contrast • MS- start immunomodulators early, meds have lots of side effects Answers 1. B 2. A 3. C 4. A 5. B 6. D</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  At least two attacks fulfilling criteria B and C</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  One or more fully reversible aura symptoms</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  At least three of the following:</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Not better accounted for by another ICHD-3 diagnosis.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 26</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="40" after="120" /><shd val="clear" fill="7F4F24" color="auto" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="54544B81" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="EBF3FB" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0"
