for the ankle</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Avoidance of use of NSAIDs</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Radiographs of the ankle</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 33</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="40" after="120" /><shd val="clear" fill="7F4F24" color="auto" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="B4A16F70" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="EBF3FB" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  [CORE]  Authors/Task Force members, Elliott PM, Anastasakis A, et al. 2014 ESC guidelines on diagnosis and management of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: the task force for the diagnosis and management of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy of the European Society of Cardiology (ESC). Eur Heart J. 2014;35(39):2733-2779.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="CBF81A3B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="EBF3FB" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Cited by: Q2024-953</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="80077AAA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="EBF3FB" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  [CORE]  Leggit JC, Whitaker D. Diagnosis and management of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: updated guidelines from the ACC/AHA. Am Fam Physician. 2022;105(2):207-209.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="37B19D82" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="EBF3FB" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Cited by: Q2024-953</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4E409800" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="EBF3FB" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  [CORE]  Rice SG; American Academy of Pediatrics Council on Sports Medicine and Fitness. Medical conditions affecting sports participation. Pediatrics . 2008;121(4):841- 848</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="BA1FC30C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="EBF3FB" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Cited by: Q2025-039</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1CA0DF26" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="EBF3FB" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  [CORE]  Bernhardt DT, Roberts WO, eds; American Academy of Family Physicians. PPE: Preparticipation Physical Evaluation . 5th ed. American Academy of Pediatrics; 2019.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0DBB754A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="EBF3FB" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Cited by: Q2025-039</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="B926FBA5" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="EBF3FB" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  [CORE]  Achar S, Yamanaka J: Apophysitis and osteochondrosis: Common causes of pain in growing bones. Am Fam Physician 2019;99(10):610-618.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="41D89CD7" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="EBF3FB" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Cited by: Q2022-587</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="D33FAE5C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><pBdr><bottom val="single" sz="4" color="1F3864" /></pBdr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="1F3864" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="4" /><szCs val="4" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="4" /><szCs val="4" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve" /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="46094F94" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFFFFF" /><spacing before="60" after="60" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve" /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="D0883197" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFFFFF" /><spacing before="60" after="60" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve" /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="45109EFE" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="000B00BD" rsidRDefault="000B00BD"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFFFFF" /><spacing before="60" after="60" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><color val="000000" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /></rPr></pPr></p><p ns2:paraId="29B03ECE" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>A. The Preparticipation Physical Evaluation (PPE)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="69663814" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Primary Objectives &amp; Counseling</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="11824FE7" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Goal: Screen individuals for factors that may predispose them to injury or be life-threatening/disabling.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4F401554" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>The AMA Directive: The American Medical Association explicitly calls for physicians to utilize the PPE to have discussions about health-related topics and risk-taking behaviors (smoking, drinking, drugs, and sex) since these athletes are primarily a healthy population that rarely interacts with the healthcare system otherwise.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="687F0B22" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Cardiovascular Screening &amp; EKG Guidelines</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6CDB0C76" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><t>[BOARD ALERT: ROUTINE EKG CONTRAINDICATION]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> The USPSTF and ACC/AHA explicitly recommend AGAINST routine cardiac screening with stress testing or EKGs in low-risk, asymptomatic individuals due to high false-positive rates and downstream harms.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2A7A0766" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>The 14-Point AHA Evaluation: The standard of care relies strictly on the AHA 14-point screening evaluation consisting of personal history, family history, and physical exam (checking for murmurs and elevated blood pressure).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="267AA9B3" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><t>[BOARD ALERT: WHEN TO ORDER AN EKG]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> An EKG prior to sports clearance is ONLY warranted if a patient has a positive result on the 14-point screening (such as elevated systemic blood pressure). Diagnostic Trap: A family history of sudden cardiac death or coronary artery bypass surgery only warrants an EKG if the event occurred in a family member prior to age 50.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4ACD031B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>ITE 2024: Question #90. Topic(s): Preparticipation evaluation / EKG indications.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="35FCFCCC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>B. Concussion Management (Mild Traumatic Brain Injury)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="457AFDB8" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Pathophysiology &amp; Sideline Evaluation</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1A644CFE" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Principle: A concussion is strictly a functional problem, not a structural problem. Routine imaging (CT/MRI) is not indicated unless severe injury is evident (e.g., repeated vomiting, severe disorientation, focal neurologic deficit) to rule out acute intracranial hemorrhage.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="14EA7B40" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Sideline Tool:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> The Sport Concussion Assessment Tool (SCAT6) is the single best and most validated instrument for the sideline evaluation of athletes.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="02870AF7" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>The Paradigm Shift: Active Recovery vs Strict Rest</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="11BC4880" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><t>[BOARD ALERT: ACTIVE RECOVERY]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Historical Trap: Complete, prolonged physical and cognitive rest (the “dark room” therapy) is NO longer recommended as it actually prolongs symptoms. New Standard: Initiate a brief rest period of 24–48 hours, followed by early, light aerobic activity (walking, stationary bike) that strictly does NOT exacerbate symptoms. Patients recover faster with early light activity.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7F3614C4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Return-to-Play Protocol</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="58C791DF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>[BOARD ALERT: RETURN TO LEARN]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> An athlete MUST successfully tolerate a return to school (“Return to Learn”) before they are permitted to resume full physical activity or sports. Crucially, they do not need to be completely symptom-free to return to the classroom.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7B33FCC9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Progression: Requires a stepwise progression. The athlete must be medically evaluated and cleared by a physician specifically before returning to full contact gameplay.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1475B6FD" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>ITE 2024: Question #102. Topic(s): Concussion management / Return to learn.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="02F9C714" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>C. Exertional Heat Illnesses</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="43570276" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Heat Cramps vs Heat Stroke</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7C064B8C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Heat Stroke:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Characterized by a core temperature &gt;104°F AND central nervous system dysfunction (altered mental status, inability to speak/function). Severe malaise or generalized weakness alone does not constitute CNS dysfunction.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="188D332C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Heat Cramps:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Common, self-limited spasms (often gastrocnemius). Treated with isotonic fluids, stretching, and massage.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7BF0D574" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Return-to-Play Timing</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6139C55D" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><t>[BOARD ALERT: IMMEDIATE RETURN]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> For simple heat-related muscle cramps, the athlete may safely resume participation immediately upon resolution of their symptoms. Conversely, heat exhaustion requires prolonged removal from play.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0055754E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>ITE 2020: Question #134. Topic(s): Heat illness / Return to play.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6981CFC1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>D. The Female Athlete Triad &amp; Eating Disorders</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="340C1594" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Epidemiology &amp; Team Approach</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4EA4974C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Athletes possess a significantly higher prevalence of eating disorders than non-athletes (18-20% of female athletes, up to 8% of male athletes).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1B4D11DB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Highly comorbid with depression, anxiety, OCD, and substance abuse disorder. Management strictly mandates a multidisciplinary team approach (medical provider, dietician, mental health professional).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="76B2D0D4" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="45023FCA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Presentation:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Amenorrhea, hair loss, low BMI, bradycardia, hypotension, euthyroid sick syndrome, and decreased serum calcium.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0255FC15" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><t>[BOARD ALERT: MEASURING RECOVERY]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> While weight restoration and normalizing lab values are critical, the return of spontaneous menses is the absolute best physiologic indication of bone mineral density (BMD) recovery in an adolescent with RED-S.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="37B460FC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>ITE 2023: Question #105. Topic(s): Female athlete triad / Bone mineral density.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1DF97599" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>E. Common Sports Orthopedics &amp; Injury Patterns</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="06213518" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Epidemiology of High School Injuries</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="42FE8A46" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>[BOARD ALERT: INJURY STATISTICS]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Football carries the absolute highest overall injury rate among high school sports, and the Knee is the most commonly injured body part across all high school athletics.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3A0B6DE1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Anterior Shoulder Dislocation</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="63833165" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><t>[BOARD ALERT: REDUCTION LOCATION]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> If an athlete suffers an anterior shoulder dislocation during a game, the most appropriate management is immediate reduction on the field. This is technically easier prior to severe muscle spasm and spares the patient the risks of hospital-based conscious sedation. You MUST perform and document a neurovascular exam of the upper extremity both before and after the reduction.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3B0AD5AB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Femoral Acetabular Impingement (FAI)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="29B3E321" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Physical Exam Trap:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Differentiate from SI joint pathology. FAI is assessed using the FAdIR test (Flexion, Adduction, Internal Rotation) — do not confuse this with FABER.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="751D32F1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Diagnostic Confirmation:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> If FAI is suspected, perform a diagnostic lidocaine injection directly into the hip joint. Immediate symptom resolution essentially confirms the diagnosis prior to obtaining an MRI.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7C382C1A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Adolescent Idiopathic Scoliosis in Athletes</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1200D8A9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><t>[BOARD ALERT: SPORTS CLEARANCE]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> If an asymptomatic adolescent athlete is found to have mild scoliosis (Cobb angle &lt;20°) during a screening or PPE, the appropriate management is observation only. You must explicitly NOT suspend them from sports participation, as suspension provides no physical benefit and contributes to psychological distress.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="45847E92" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>ITE 2021: Question #177. Topic(s): Adolescent scoliosis / Sports clearance.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="06F88334" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="0066644A" rsidRDefault="00000000"><pPr><pStyle val="Heading1" /></pPr><r><t>Session 34: Pediatric Orthopedics</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1E42945E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><pBdr><top val="single" sz="12" color="1F3864" /></pBdr><shd Syndrome</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="15D3BB51" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="2E75B6" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /></pPr><r><rPr><b /><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="20" /><szCs val="20" /><color val="FFFFFF" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  AAFP Poll Questions — Session 34: Pediatric Orthopedics</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="0" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="7F4F24" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  8 practice questions from the AAFP Board Prep presentation. Work each question before checking the answer key.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  1.  Which of the following statements is true regarding bone tumors in the pediatric &amp; adolescent population?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Ewing’s sarcoma is the most common malignant bone tumor</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Osteochondroma is the most common benign bone tumor in</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Osteosarcoma most commonly arises in the spine or pelvis</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Non-ossifying fibromas resolve with skeletal maturity</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 34</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  2.  A 6 yo female is brought to your office for evaluation. She was born at 39 3/7 weeks by NSVD without complication. She has met all orthopedic developmental milestones. Her mother notes that she has been more clumsy at recess, occasionally falling when running. On examination you note that her patellae point inward, and her feet point inward, and there is no joint or limb tenderness or swelling; her neurological exam is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  External tibial torsion</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Internal tibial torsion</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Femoral anteversion</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Femoral retroversion</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 34</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  3.  A 15 yo male is brought into your office for evaluation of a new limp. He plays football as an offensive lineman and noted onset after practice 3 days ago. He has not been ill and no recent immunizations. Examination demonstrates T 98.6⁰ F, P 74, Ht 72”, Wt200 lbs, antalgic gait, and limited and painful ROM in the left hip. What is your presumptive diagnosis?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Legg-Calve-Perthes</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Transient synovitis of the hip</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Slipped capital femoral epiphysis</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Osteochondritis dissecans of the knee</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 34</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  4.  A 7 yo female is brought to the ED with right hip pain and a new limp. Onset has been abrupt in the last 12 hours. No known trauma or injury. She is otherwise well and no recent illness or immunizations. On examination you note a T 39.2⁰ C, normal respirations, pulse &amp; BP. The right hip appears normal, is painful with ROM, and she limps with ambulation. Hip radiographs are normal. Which of the following is most useful in determining your treatment plan?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  CBC alone</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  CBC and ESR</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Aspiration of the hip joint</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  CBC, ESR and CRP</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 34</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  5.  A 12 yo male is brought in for evaluation of his right elbow pain. He is a pitcher on his middle school baseball team. Onset was insidious, occurred about 3 weeks into his baseball season, and is aggravated by throwing and relieved by rest. He has noted decreased velocity and accuracy of his pitches. On exam you note tenderness at the right medial epicondyle. You conclude the following: Traction Apophysitis • Insidious onset that results from overuseof the [tendon and] surrounding ossification centers Rapid increase in training Consistent high intensity training Limited recovery time • Boys 10-14 years of age most commonly affected • Often occurs at or after growth spurt • Physical activity required • These are clinical diagnoses and radiographs are not neededin the appropriate clinical setting Little League Elbow Baseball players and Their Shoulder Injuries; Houston Clinic Health Alert • Progressive medial elbow pain, ▼ throwing accuracy/speed/distance • Refrain from throwing for 3-6 weeks until pain-free &amp; nontenderthen progressive return-to-throwing program with focus on proper throwing mechanics • Major League Baseball preventive measures = 4 months rest per year, no radar guns, and avoid playing pitcher and catcher Caution Overhead athletes with open growth plates at risk: Swimming Think of this as a stress Baseball fracture of the proximal Volleyball Tennis humeral physis Gymnastics</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  This is a lateral epicondylar stress fracture</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  This is an apophysitis</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  This is golfer’s elbow</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  This is tennis elbow</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 34</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  6.  An 11 yo male is brought to your office for evaluation of bilateral posterior heel pain that has occurred for the past few months. He plays basketball and soccer several times a week, and the pain begins several minutes into each of these activities. There is no pain at rest or with walking. He denies numbness, tingling, or weakness. On exam you find no swelling or tenderness of the heel or Achilles tendon. Reflexes, strength, and range of motion at the ankle are intact, but he does have bilateral posterior heel pain when you passively and fully dorsiflex the ankles.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 34</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  7.  Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Plantar fasciitis</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Sever’s disease</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Heel pad syndrome</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Achilles tendonopathy</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 34</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  8.  Pediatric obesity is associated with each of the following except: repair Skeletal Impact of Obesity Pediatric &amp; Adolescent Fracture Delayed fracture healing Surgical complications of fracture repair Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis Blount’s disease Flat feet Low back pain and herniated disc disease Arthritis Impaired mobility Answers 1.D 2.C 3.C 4.D 5.B 6.B 7.B Supplemental Slides Pediatric Movement Disorders Cerebral Palsy Brain insult due to hemorrhage, infection, toxin, or anoxia Most common childhood physical disability Spastic type (70%) is most common ►stiff, exaggerated movements Nonprogressive Diagnosis @ 18 mo–5 years Club Foot • Males &gt; females • ~80% detected by US at 24 weeks gestation Reproduced with permission from Ortholnfo. © American Academy of OrthopaedicSurgeons, http://orthoinfo/aaos.org • Management includes serial casting [Ponseti method] • Surgical correction occurs at 9-12 months, if needed Osteochondritis Dissecans of the Knee • ♂ &gt; ♀, average age 10-20 years, bilateral in 30-40% • 85% occur in the medial epicondyle &amp; 70% at the lateral aspect of the medial epicondyle (LAME) • Radiographs as initial test (tunnel or notch view) MRI can demonstrate degree of involvement • • Girls &lt; 11 &amp; boys &lt; 13 usually do well without surgery if fragment intact Osgood Schlatter’s Prolotherapy  RCT of conservative care vs double-blind injection of 1% lidocaine with or w/o 12.5% dextrose monthly x 3  65 knees  ♀ 9-15 yo, ♂ 10-17 yo  Prolotherapy resulted in maintaining unaltered sport (21/21 dextrose, 20/22 lidocaine, 13/22 conservative); fewer symptoms; and asymptomatic state at one year TopolGA. et al. Pediatrics 2011 Benign Nocturnal Limb Pains of Childhood “Growing Pains” • Affects 35% of 4-6 yo (can persist to age 19) • Bilateral and affects lower extremities • Deep cramping pain that often awakens from sleep • Resolves by morning • NO LIMP • Aggravated by heavy exercise during the day • Management: Reassurance, heat, massage, warm baths, exercise to non-aggravating levels, mild analgesics Uzielet al. PediatrRheumatol2007 Evans et al. J Pediatr2004</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Impaired mobility</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Overall lower risk of fracture</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs complications for fracture</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz
