after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="12" /><szCs val="12" /><color val="444444" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Cited by: Q2024-920</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7FB7CAF5" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><pBdr><bottom val="single" sz="4" color="1F3864" /></pBdr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="1F3864" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="4" /><szCs val="4" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="4" /><szCs val="4" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve" /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="749E2A52" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFFFFF" /><spacing before="60" after="60" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve" /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4E37B275" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFFFFF" /><spacing before="60" after="60" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /><color val="000000" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve" /></r></p><p ns2:paraId="44D3F0DC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="000B00BD" rsidRDefault="000B00BD"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="FFFFFF" /><spacing before="60" after="60" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><color val="000000" /><sz val="6" /><szCs val="6" /></rPr></pPr></p><p ns2:paraId="11CBF0D5" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>A. Imaging Principles in Fracture Care</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="57E2C4BD" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Modality Hierarchy</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="31A26AB1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>First-Line:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Standard Radiographs (minimum 3 views: AP, lateral, oblique). If high suspicion but negative initial films, splint/protect the extremity and repeat X-rays in 7-14 days.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3EE3CE95" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Second-Line:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> MRI without IV contrast.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="44791281" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>CT Indication:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> A Non-Contrast CT is the preferred imaging modality specifically for displaced intra-articular distal radial fractures and tibial plateau fractures to precisely plan surgical intervention and assess OA risk.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="73C7B9AC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>B. Pediatric Fractures</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="502B0161" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Salter-Harris Classifications</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1CE0D6DC" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Type I:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Straight across the physis. Mechanism + tenderness + normal X-ray = immobilize.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="789F699F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Type II:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Above the physis (into metaphysis). The most common type. Immobilize.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="16A3DF57" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Type III (Lower/Epiphysis) &amp; Type IV (Through/Both):</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Intra-articular fractures. Must refer for surgical evaluation (ORIF).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="27BBD9B5" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Type V:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Crush injury. Arrests growth. Requires surgical referral.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6C9BCB12" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Supracondylar Fracture (Elbow)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2FCA54F8" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Occurs in 5-7 year olds via FOOSH.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="002A8970" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Nerve Entrapment:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> The Anterior Interosseous Nerve (AIN) is most commonly involved. Test by asking the child to make an “OK” sign. Also test radial (wrist extension) and median function.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7AE72AE8" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Radiographic Clues:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> An anterior fat pad suggests a fracture. A posterior fat pad mandates initial treatment as a presumed fracture (pathognomonic for fluid pushing the capsule out).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="11660058" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Clinical Clue:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> The inability to fully extend the elbow is highly associated with elbow fractures.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1127EDCA" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Management:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Non-displaced (Type I) requires a long arm cast for 3 weeks with repeat X-rays at 1 week. Any displacement requires immediate orthopedic referral for reduction and percutaneous pinning.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="43DA0C3D" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Buckle (Torus) Fracture</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1F14604C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Accounts for 50% of pediatric wrist fractures.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6BBEF8CE" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><t>[BOARD ALERT: MANAGEMENT]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Place in a removable splint/brace for 3 weeks. Do NOT cast, and do NOT perform follow-up X-rays for isolated, non-angulated distal radial buckle fractures if the child has reliable parents.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="185BF5B1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Nursemaid’s Elbow (Radial Head Subluxation)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1AED4EB6" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Occurs in 2-3 year olds via a traction injury. Child holds elbow in extension and pronation.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1CF15391" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>[BOARD ALERT: REDUCTION TECHNIQUE]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Hyperpronation of the forearm requires fewer attempts and is statistically more successful initially than the classic supination/flexion maneuver.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="711FA509" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>C. Upper Extremity Adult Fractures</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="660B2F6C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Clavicle Fractures</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="56272477" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>The most commonly fractured bone. 85% occur in the middle third.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="36AEC817" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Management: A simple sling is functionally equivalent to a figure-8 brace. Surgical referral is required for skin tenting, neurovascular compromise, or overriding fragments &gt; 2 cm.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6A3EF28B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Distal Radial Fractures</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0BEBB6AF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Make up 16% of all fractures. Driven by osteoporosis and older age.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6CCC1889" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Nerve Entrapment:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> The Median nerve is the most commonly injured nerve.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6CD62C1D" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><t>[BOARD ALERT: OA RISK]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> 1-2 mm of articular incongruity drastically raises the risk of osteoarthritis, mandating a non-contrast CT and surgical evaluation.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6F67478C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Scaphoid Fracture</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="6830BF2B" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Presentation:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> FOOSH injury with focal tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3122B175" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><t>[BOARD ALERT: MANAGEMENT RULE]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Trauma + snuffbox tenderness + normal initial X-rays = Apply a thumb spica splint and repeat X-rays in 2 weeks.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1AFEB15E" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Complications:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> The blood supply is distal-to-proximal. The more proximal the fracture, the higher the risk of non-union and avascular necrosis. Non-displaced fractures are casted; displaced fractures require surgical fixation.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="37E73126" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Specific Finger Injuries</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3B555C04" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Jersey Finger:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Avulsion of the flexor digitorum profundus (FDP) tendon at the distal phalanx. Patient cannot actively flex the DIP. Requires surgical correction within 10-12 days (before the tendon permanently retracts).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="54B7DDCB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Boutonniere Deformity:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Central slip extensor tendon injury at the PIP. Splint the PIP joint in full extension for 6 weeks, followed by night splinting for 4-6 weeks.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2CC49482" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Mallet Finger:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Avulsion of the extensor digitorum tendon at the DIP. Patient cannot actively extend the distal phalanx. Splint the DIP joint in full extension continuously for 6-8 weeks. (If the patient removes the splint early, the 6-8 week clock restarts entirely).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="0C81F7F3" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>D. Fragility Fractures (Osteoporosis)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7111EC02" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Definition &amp; Required Workup</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5797F48C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>A fracture resulting from a fall from a standing height (or less) in a postmenopausal woman or a man &gt; 70 years old.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7B8D8762" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><t>[BOARD ALERT: MANDATORY NEXT STEP]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> The presence of a fragility fracture mandates a bone health evaluation via a DXA scan.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7F8FA359" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Vertebral Compression Fractures (VCF)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="447A8B23" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>Occur primarily at T8-L2 and L4. 66-67% remain undiagnosed as elderly patients attribute the pain to normal aging.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2C3896A8" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Mortality: Women with a VCF carry a 15% higher mortality rate due to the development of restrictive pulmonary disease (from kyphosis) or underlying malignancy.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="11F875D0" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Management:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Early mobilization is critical. Utilize analgesics, including Calcitonin nasal spray (specifically indicated for osteoporotic VCF pain).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="03CC6AAF" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Pharmacology Pearl:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Do not exceed 5 years of long-term bisphosphonate treatment to avoid atypical femur fractures.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="54255EF1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Pelvic Insufficiency Fractures</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="38B81333" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Occurs in postmenopausal women and men &gt; 75. 2/3 occur in the strict absence of trauma (e.g., sitting down heavily on a bed).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="08C56898" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Management:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Obtain a baseline hemoglobin (retroperitoneal bleeding risk). Hospital admission is often required for pain control to allow early mobilization. The average length of stay in rehab/transitional care is 14-45 days.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="184A3343" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Hip Fractures</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="719D52F9" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><t>[BOARD ALERT: PROGNOSIS]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> 1/3 of previously independent patients will be dead within 1 year of the fracture. Another 30% will become permanently dependent in a nursing facility.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="3B851F28" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><spacing before="220" after="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>E. Lower Extremity Trauma &amp; Stress Fractures</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="7D090F61" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><spacing before="220" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Ottawa Ankle Rules</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="5F9FF1E0" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Used to rule out the need for X-rays. Order X-rays strictly if there is an inability to bear weight for 4 steps at the time of exam, OR discrete bony tenderness at the posterior edge/tip of the lateral or medial malleolus (Ankle series), OR tenderness at the navicular or base of the 5th metatarsal (Foot series).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="160EFF1C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Pediatric Rule: Do not order ankle/midfoot X-rays for patients &gt; 6 years old without meeting these specific criteria.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="585D9BC1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>5th Metatarsal Fractures</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="1A2D1900" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Tuberosity Avulsion:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Most common. Treated with splinting/boot and progressive weight-bearing as tolerated.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="4F24DF54" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><t>[BOARD ALERT: JONES FRACTURE]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> Jones Fracture (Metaphyseal-Diaphyseal Junction): High rate of non-union (50%). Requires 4-6 weeks of strict non-weight-bearing, or surgical screw fixation.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="54987A8C" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="0" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t>Stress Fractures</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="78A22F50" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Epidemiology:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> The Tibia is the most common site (50%), followed by the metatarsals (25%), and fibula (10%).</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="00FE03D5" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /></rPr><t>Imaging Hierarchy:</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> 1) Radiographs. 2) Repeat Radiographs in 10-14 days. 3) MRI without contrast. 4) Bone scan.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2BE5A7EB" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="001E0BC7" rsidRDefault="00000000" rsidP="00B71DEE"><pPr><keepNext /><keepLines /><numPr><ilvl val="1" /><numId val="264" /></numPr><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /></rPr><lastRenderedPageBreak /><t>[BOARD ALERT: DREADED BLACK LINE]</t></r><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve"> An anterior mid-tibial stress fracture. Carries a 50% non-union rate. Requires prolonged non-weight-bearing and often surgical intramedullary rod placement.</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="2467BC4A" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="0066644A" rsidRDefault="00000000"><pPr><pStyle val="Heading1" /></pPr><r><t>Session 35: Common ENT Problems</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="86017CE1" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><pBdr><top val="single" sz="12" val="444444" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="444444" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Diagnostic workup of episodic vertigo with audiovestibular symptoms Meniere's Disease Diagnosis</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="857AB45D" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="D6E4F7" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="1F3864" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="1F3864" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Q5   2025   •   Q2025-065   (match score: 1.93, 5 keyword hits)</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="46AA4DFD" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="D6E4F7" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><ind left="180" right="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="444444" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><i /><iCs /><sz val="14" /><szCs val="14" /><color val="444444" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  Peripheral vertigo management approach Vestibular Disorders</t></r></p><p ns2:paraId="B2592A5F" ns2:textId="77777777" rsidR="00CC0000" rsidRDefault="00CC0000"><pPr><shd val="clear" color="auto" fill="2E75B6" /><spacing before="0" after="0" /><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="16" /><szCs val="16" /><color val="FFFFFF" /></rPr></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Aptos" hAnsi="Aptos" /><b /><bCs /><sz val="16" /><szCs val="16" /><color val="FFFFFF" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  KEY REFERENCES</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="120" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="7F4F24" color="auto" /></pPr><r><rPr><b /><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="20" /><szCs val="20" /><color val="FFFFFF" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  AAFP Poll Questions — Session 35: Common ENT Problems</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="0" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="7F4F24" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  6 practice questions from the AAFP Board Prep presentation. Work each question before checking the answer key.</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  1.  A 12-month-old male patient is brought into your office by his mother for fever, cough and pulling on his left ear. He is afebrile in your office, playful and interactive. When you examine his ear, the tympanic membranes are slightly erythematous, but he is uncooperative for pneumatic otoscopy. Your diagnosis is?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  URI, acute otitis media uncertain</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Otitis media with effusion</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Acute otitis media</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Ramsay Hunt syndrome</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 35</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  2.  A 22-month-old male patient is brought to you crying and in obvious acute distress from right ear pain. He has a fever of 103.6 and on exam has an immobile, bulging, erythematous right TM. The best treatment option would be?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Do not use antibiotics because this is probably a viral illness</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Have the parents observe for 48 hours and treat with</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Treat with antihistamines/decongestants alone</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Start antibiotics immediately</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 35</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  3.  You diagnose AOM with severe symptoms in a 22-month-old male patient who weighs 20 kg (44 lb). What is the most appropriate treatment?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Amoxicillin 900 mg/day</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Amoxicillin 1800 mg/day</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 900 mg/day</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 1800 mg/day</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 35</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  4.  You treated a child with AOM with antibiotics and see him back 4 weeks later. The child is asymptomatic, but you determine he has a middle ear effusion. Your recommendation would be?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Reassurance and reevaluate in 2 months</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Oral antihistamine for 30 days</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Re-treat with amoxicillin-clavulanate</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Nasal steroid for 30 days</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 35</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  5.  A 43-year-old male presents to your office with five days of nasal congestion and headache. His temperature is 100.8oF, he has purulent rhinorrhea, and minimal tenderness to palpation over the frontal and maxillary sinuses. Your next step would be?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Transilluminate the sinuses</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      B)  Get sinus x-rays</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Treat with nasal saline and topical steroids</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Treat with amoxicillin-clavulanate</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs val="15" /><color val="888888" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      Poll question — AAFP Board Prep Session 35</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="80" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="180" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="18" /><szCs val="18" /><color val="1A1A1A" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">  6.  A 57-year-old female patient of yours presents with dizziness and a sensation that she is spinning. It occurs when she turns in bed or lifts her head to look in an upper cabinet. Episodes are brief but are becoming more frequent. She has no tinnitus or hearing loss. The most likely diagnosis would be?</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      A)  Meniere’s Disease</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      C)  Vestibular neuronitis</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="0" /><shd val="clear" fill="FAE3A0" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="17" /><szCs val="17" /><color val="3D3D3D" /></rPr><t xml:space="preserve">      D)  Acoustic neuroma</t></r></p><p><pPr><spacing before="20" after="60" /><shd val="clear" fill="FFF2CC" color="auto" /><ind left="360" /></pPr><r><rPr><rFonts ascii="Arial" hAnsi="Arial" /><sz val="15" /><szCs
